concise:
Ayah 1–10 · linked video: 01. ’Abasa (Ayah 1-10)

Surah Abasa: Understanding True Worth and Priority

Ayah 1

عَبَسَ وَتَوَلَّىٰٓ

He frowned and turned away.

This surah opens with a remarkable scene from the life of the Prophet ﷺ - one that teaches profound lessons about priorities, dignity and how we should view others. The Prophet ﷺ was engaged in conversation with some of the elite leaders of Quraysh. These were the wealthy, influential figures who typically opposed him but were, for once, actually listening. This was a rare opportunity, and the Prophet ﷺ understood something important from the examples of previous messengers: reaching influential people can open doors for the message to spread more widely.

We see this pattern throughout Quranic stories. Prophet Musa (AS) was sent first to Pharaoh before addressing the broader nation. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was sent to Makkah - Umm al-Qura, the mother of all towns - because it was the center of influence in Arabia. There is wisdom in speaking to those who hold positions of power and influence in society.

As this conversation was taking place, Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA) - a blind companion and cousin of the Prophet ﷺ - heard the Prophet’s voice and came to him. Unable to see that the Prophet ﷺ was already engaged in discussion, he eagerly asked, “Ya Rasulallah, teach me something from what Allah has taught you.” His enthusiasm was pure and sincere. He wasn’t bringing an emergency or a specific problem - he simply wanted to learn something beneficial from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

When someone is genuinely excited to learn, they can sometimes lose awareness of social norms around them. Abdullah (RA) wasn’t being deliberately rude or inconsiderate - he was simply overcome with eagerness to benefit from the Prophet’s guidance. The Prophet ﷺ found himself in a difficult position: he didn’t want to dismiss Abdullah (RA) or hurt his feelings, but he also didn’t want to lose this rare opportunity with the Qurayshi leaders.

The word عَبَسَ describes the very slightest frown - just a small furrow between the eyebrows, barely visible. This wasn’t anger or harsh displeasure. It was a momentary expression of internal conflict about how to handle two audiences at once. The Prophet ﷺ then turned his attention back toward the leaders, asking them, “Do you see any problem with what I’ve shared with you so far?” - a gentle way of maintaining the conversation while subtly indicating to Abdullah (RA) that a discussion was already in progress.

What’s remarkable is that Allah ﷻ addresses even this slightest reaction. The Prophet ﷺ didn’t speak harshly to Abdullah (RA). He didn’t dismiss him rudely. Abdullah (RA), being blind, couldn’t even see the frown. Yet Allah ﷻ corrects this moment because of what it represents about our priorities and values.

Ayah 2

أَن جَآءَهُ ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ

Because the blind man came to him.

Notice how Allah ﷻ describes the situation: “He frowned and turned away” - but turned away from whom? In that scene, you might naturally describe it as “he turned toward the important leaders.” But Allah ﷻ flips the perspective entirely: “he turned away from the blind man.”

This is the Quran’s way of redefining who truly matters. In society’s eyes, the leaders were the VIPs. But in Allah’s sight, the one who deserves attention and honor is Abdullah (RA). By specifically mentioning that he was blind - الْأَعْمَىٰ - Allah ﷻ isn’t being degrading. Rather, He is highlighting the additional effort this man made to reach the Prophet ﷺ.

When you are blind, every journey requires more effort. Finding your way, navigating obstacles, relying on sound to locate people - all of this demands extra determination. That he came at all is itself praiseworthy. Moreover, people with disabilities often face social isolation. They don’t have the same opportunities to interact, to learn, to participate. When they do get a chance to connect with knowledge and guidance, they value it immensely.

The Quran is establishing a universal principle here: those who face challenges and still strive to learn deserve special consideration and honor. Our masajid must be accessible. Our programs must accommodate those with different needs. We cannot treat people with disabilities as afterthoughts or inconveniences.

Ayah 3

وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّهُۥ يَزَّكَّىٰٓ

And what would make you perceive that perhaps he might be purified?

Allah ﷻ asks the Prophet ﷺ: How could you know what’s in his heart? The word يُدْرِيكَ refers to having clues or indicators that lead to discovery. Allah ﷻ is saying: you don’t have the tools to see what I see. You cannot judge someone’s sincerity, their potential for growth, or their inner reality based on outward appearances.

The phrase لَعَلَّهُۥ يَزَّكَّىٰٓ - “perhaps he might be purified” - is beautiful in its humility. The verb form يَزَّكَّىٰ suggests beginning the process of purification, taking even the first steps toward becoming better. Sometimes one powerful reminder, one meaningful conversation, is enough to start someone on a journey of transformation. You may not see immediate change, but something begins internally.

This challenges our tendency to judge people based on their social standing, appearance, or how they present themselves. Someone may seem socially awkward or may not follow normal conversational protocols, but what if their heart is sincere? What if they genuinely want to grow? We have no way of knowing unless we give them the benefit of the doubt and treat them with dignity.

Ayah 4

أَوْ يَذَّكَّرُ فَتَنفَعَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰٓ

Or be reminded and the reminder benefit him?

Even if immediate transformation doesn’t happen, perhaps the reminder will linger. The word يَذَّكَّرُ means to take something into your mind and heart and reflect on it over time. Change doesn’t always happen instantly. Sometimes you hear something and it sits with you, works on you internally. You think about it, and gradually it reshapes your perspective and behavior.

This is like a seed planted underground. For a while, you see nothing on the surface. But roots are forming, a foundation is being established. Eventually, growth becomes visible. Many of us can recall advice we received years ago - from parents, teachers, friends - that we couldn’t fully appreciate at the time. But later in life, when circumstances aligned, those words came back and benefited us deeply.

The reminder - الذِّكْرَىٰ - carries benefit even if that benefit is delayed. This is why regular reminders matter. This is why every salah, every khutbah, every gathering of knowledge has value. We never know which word will be the seed that eventually blossoms into positive change.

These ayahs emphasize that we should never withhold guidance or knowledge from someone based on assumptions about whether they’ll benefit from it. That’s not for us to determine. Our job is to share, to teach, to remind - and leave the results to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 5

أَمَّا مَنِ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰ

As for he who considers himself without need…

Now Allah ﷻ turns the attention to the other party in this scene - the elite leaders of Quraysh. The word ٱسْتَغْنَىٰ comes from غنى, meaning wealth and self-sufficiency. But اسْتَغْنَىٰ in the استفعال form means to feel or consider oneself to be above needing anything or anyone. It describes those who see themselves as independent, as if everyone depends on them but they depend on no one.

This isn’t just about material wealth. It’s an attitude of the heart. “What does Islam offer me? I already have everything - health, family, success, happiness. Why do I need religion?” This mindset reduces human existence to physical and emotional needs while denying the reality of spiritual needs.

We are tripartite beings: physical, emotional and spiritual. These dimensions are interconnected. If you’re physically unwell, it affects your emotions. If you’re emotionally distressed, it affects your spirituality. But many people, when their physical and emotional needs seem satisfied, assume they have no need for spiritual fulfillment. They’re blind to that hunger within them.

The attitude of استغنى is particularly dangerous because it closes the door to guidance. Someone who recognizes their need will seek help. Someone who thinks they have everything already won’t even look for what’s missing.

Ayah 6

فَأَنتَ لَهُۥ تَصَدَّىٰ

Then you give him attention.

The word تَصَدَّىٰ is powerful. It comes from the image of a bird that relentlessly chirps from branch to branch, or an echo that keeps returning. Allah ﷻ is describing how the Prophet ﷺ was persistently trying to get and maintain the attention of these leaders.

The phrase فَأَنتَ لَهُۥ - “you, for him” - emphasizes the Prophet’s focus. You, of all people - the Messenger of Allah ﷻ, the one who carries divine revelation - you are pursuing him? You are going out of your way for his attention?

This corrects our understanding of dignity and worth. The Prophet ﷺ doesn’t need to chase anyone’s approval or attention. The message he carries is infinitely more valuable than any worldly status. When we understand this, we realize that it should be the other way around: people of influence should be eager to sit with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, not the other way around.

Ayah 7

وَمَا عَلَيْكَ أَلَّا يَزَّكَّىٰ

And not upon you is any blame if he will not be purified.

This ayah contains a subtle but crucial shift. If it had said “you won’t be held responsible if he doesn’t purify himself,” it would mean the outcome is uncertain. But by saying أَلَّا يَزَّكَّىٰ, Allah ﷻ is informing the Prophet ﷺ: he won’t be purifying himself. I’m letting you know what’s in his heart.

Only Allah ﷻ knows what lies within someone’s heart. Only He can make such declarations. We don’t have access to that knowledge, which is why we must always give people the benefit of the doubt and never write anyone off. But when Allah ﷻ informs His Messenger ﷺ of someone’s spiritual reality, it’s definitive.

This is a reminder not to waste precious energy on those who have closed their hearts while neglecting those who are genuinely seeking. Your responsibility is to deliver the message, not to force hearts to change.

Ayah 8

وَأَمَّا مَن جَآءَكَ يَسْعَىٰ

But as for he who came to you striving…

Now we return to Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA), and Allah ﷻ reveals what the Prophet ﷺ couldn’t see: the internal reality of this man’s heart. The word يَسْعَىٰ means to exert effort, to rush toward something with determination.

This echoes themes from the previous surah: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ - “That Day, man will remember what he strove for” (79:35). The concept of striving, of effort, of sa’y is highlighted. Abdullah (RA) made the effort to come. He navigated the difficulty of his blindness to reach the Prophet ﷺ. That effort itself is praiseworthy.

Ayah 9

وَهُوَ يَخْشَىٰ

While he fears Allah.

This is the divine stamp of approval. وَهُوَ يَخْشَىٰ - and he is one who fears Allah ﷻ. This is Allah ﷻ Himself testifying to what’s in Abdullah’s heart. Only Allah ﷻ truly knows who has خشية - that profound, reverential fear mixed with awe and love.

Fear of Allah ﷻ is what makes someone truly valuable. Not wealth, not status, not fame, not lineage. In an earlier surah, Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ: إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرُ مَن يَخْشَىٰهَا - “You are only a warner for those who fear it [the Day of Judgment]” (79:45). Your role is to serve those who have this fear. And here is someone with that fear standing right in front of you.

This is an immense honor for Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA). To have Allah ﷻ declare in His eternal Book that you are among those who fear Him - what greater certification could exist? After this revelation, the Prophet ﷺ always gave Abdullah (RA) special attention and honor. He would ask him, “Is there anything you need? Can I do anything for you?” And when Abdullah would arrive or leave, the Prophet ﷺ would make sure he was well taken care of. The Prophet ﷺ even appointed him to be in charge of Madinah on multiple occasions. This is how the Prophet ﷺ corrected that one moment for the rest of his life.

Ayah 10

فَأَنتَ عَنْهُ تَلَهَّىٰ

From him you are distracted.

The word تَلَهَّىٰ means to be distracted or preoccupied by something else. Allah ﷻ is saying: this person - the one who came striving, the one who fears Me - you allowed yourself to be distracted from him.

Again, the emphasis on فَأَنتَ - “you, of all people” - highlights the magnitude of the lesson. If even the Prophet ﷺ needed this correction, how much more do we need it?

The deeper lesson here transcends this specific incident. It’s about how we establish value and priority in our communities. When a mayor or governor visits the masjid, do we roll out special treatment while ignoring the regular worshippers? When wealthy donors arrive, do we fawn over them while dismissing volunteers who serve tirelessly? When celebrities or influential figures show interest in Islam, do we elevate them while making others feel invisible?

Islam came to eliminate class hierarchies. In salah, we stand shoulder to shoulder regardless of our worldly status. The doctor and the day laborer, the CEO and the student, the famous and the unknown - we all bow the same way, prostrate the same way, stand with equal humility before Allah ﷻ. This isn’t just a spiritual exercise; it’s training for how we should treat each other in all aspects of life.

The message is clear: true worth is determined by تقوى - consciousness and fear of Allah ﷻ. Not by wealth, power, influence or fame. Every person who enters our masajid, every seeker of knowledge, every sincere heart deserves respect and dignity. We don’t get to decide who is more valuable based on worldly measures.

This surah opened with a scene involving the most noble human being who ever lived ﷺ being told that a blind man deserves to be treated as a VIP. If that was the standard set for him, imagine what standard should be set for us. May Allah ﷻ help us internalize these lessons and transform how we see and treat every human being.

Ayah 11–42 · linked video: 02. ’Abasa (Ayah 11-42)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 11

Ayah 11

كَلَّآ إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ

No! Indeed, it is a powerful reminder.

This ayah marks a decisive shift in tone. After addressing the Prophet ﷺ about his interaction with the blind companion and the wealthy leaders of Quraysh, Allah ﷻ now establishes the true status of revelation itself.

The word كَلَّآ serves as a firm rejection. It’s as if Allah ﷻ is saying to the Prophet ﷺ: Don’t you dare think for a second that you need to sell this message to anyone. Don’t worry about whether they believe or not. You’ve done your part—you conveyed what I commanded you to convey.

The Quraysh leaders wanted the Prophet ﷺ to feel desperate, to negotiate with them as if he were selling something cheap in a marketplace. When a salesperson is eager to make a sale, the customer feels empowered and acts accordingly. But Allah ﷻ declares: You are not selling anything. They should feel honored to even have access to this revelation.

The phrase إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ emphasizes that this Quran is “a powerful reminder.” A reminder is not something new—it recalls what you already knew. The Quran reminds humanity of their own history, their origins, their purpose, and where they stand before their Creator. It offers a moral and spiritual view of history, distinct from academic, nationalistic, or skewed perspectives.

This is a reminder for whoever wants it. The mission of the Prophet ﷺ is singular: to remind people by means of it. The reaction of people is not his concern. Even when he stood before the army of his enemies and declared, “Warn your closest relatives,” he fulfilled his duty regardless of the political correctness or awkwardness of the moment. There is nothing to hide, nothing to be embarrassed about. The word of Allah ﷻ is the only thing that brings honor—everything else is humiliation.

The Prophet ﷺ does not change hearts; the One who changes hearts is Allah ﷻ, Al-Muqallib al-Qulub. You can speak to a hundred people for an hour, but it is Allah ﷻ who decides when to say “kun” (be), and for that one person at that one moment, their entire life transforms. It’s not because of eloquent speech—it’s because of Allah’s decision.

This understanding liberates the believer from frustration. When you remind someone and they still behave contrary to guidance, don’t be surprised. The prophets reminded people their entire lives, and people still did what they did. Sincere effort combined with Allah’s final decision is what brings change. Some people’s lives are transformed in the most unexpected places: a car accident, a funeral, a graveyard visit. Allah ﷻ guides in different and unique ways.

There is a profound difference between decent people and wretched people. A decent person, when they don’t see results, blames themselves. A wretched person doesn’t do the work, and when they don’t see results, blames everyone and everything else—society, the weather, the economy, anything but themselves.

Ayah 12

فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ

So whoever wills may remember it.

This ayah presents an open invitation without compulsion. Whoever wants to benefit from this heavenly gift can do so. Whoever wants to dismiss it faces their own loss.

The difference between Allah ﷻ and His messengers is significant here. Allah ﷻ says, “Take it or leave it.” The messengers, on the other hand, love their people, care about them, worry about them, and beg them to listen. They are merciful toward their communities. But when people start insulting Allah’s messengers and dismissing His word, Allah ﷻ responds differently: What do you think you are? I was kind to you and kindly sent you a messenger. And you’re going to act like that? I’m going to put you in your place.

The phrase فَمَن شَآءَ indicates that whoever wants to benefit will do so according to their capacity and willingness. But that outcome is not on the messenger. His part is to deliver the message. The Prophet ﷺ, as great as he was, does not change hearts. That power belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

This ayah also addresses those who feel they don’t need guidance. It tells them: whoever wants can access it, but we’re not going to appease you or conform to your expectations. You don’t have to be impressed with Islam for it to be true. The Prophet ﷺ was the most impressive human being who ever lived—known for honesty, integrity, and character. Yet he was demonized, and there was Islamophobia in Makkah. It wasn’t because something was missing in his character or message; people will do what they’re going to do. You must be true to who you are without conforming to anyone else.

This is the essence of كَلَّآ إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ (No! It is a reminder). Those who carry the Quran must carry themselves with dignity, not desperation. You’re not desperate for anybody’s approval.

Ayah 13

فِى صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ

In honored scriptures.

Now Allah ﷻ begins to describe the elevated origin and status of this revelation. The word صُحُفٍ refers to scrolls and scriptures. The vast majority of Quraysh were unlettered, uneducated people. Now they are being educated by scrolls and scriptures—a remarkable elevation of their status.

The description مُّكَرَّمَةٍ means “dignified” or “honored.” This is strategic. The arrogant elite of Quraysh felt they carried dignity and status. But Allah ﷻ declares: You think you carry dignity? True dignity lies in these scrolls being given to you. The karamah (honor) you seek is found in revelation, not in your lineage or wealth.

Every word here carries a subtle message. Those who feel they have status are being told that the scriptures coming to them are from the highest of places. Those who feel clean and pure are being told that the scripture itself is purified, and it has come to cleanse them.

This notion of the book’s elevated status will appear repeatedly throughout the Quran. What we are reading is not just another book—it is directly connected to the heavens. Later, in other surahs, we will learn that it is located فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ (in a well-protected book), kept away like a hidden treasure in an eagle’s nest high on a mountain, inaccessible except by Allah’s permission.

Ayah 14

مَّرْفُوعَةٍ مُّطَهَّرَةٍۭ

Exalted and purified.

The description continues with two powerful attributes: مَّرْفُوعَةٍ meaning “elevated” or “raised high,” and مُّطَهَّرَةٍ meaning “purified” or “cleansed.”

For those who feel they occupy high positions in society, this revelation comes from a place far higher. For those who consider themselves above reproach, this revelation comes to purify them. The one who thinks himself elevated is told: this Book is more elevated. The one who thinks himself pure is told: this Book is here to cleanse you.

The scholars note that these descriptions address the arrogance displayed in the opening passage. The wealthy person who felt above needing guidance is now being shown his true place. Where you think you stand high, the revelation is higher. Where you think you are clean, the revelation has come to cleanse you.

This sets the proper attitude for approaching the Quran. We are dealing with something very high, very noble, and very sacred. We’re dealing with something we would never have had access to on our own. There was no way for us to reach these words coming from Allah ﷻ. Every word of the Quran should bring us to humility.

Ayah 15

بِأَيْدِى سَفَرَةٍ

In the hands of messenger-angels.

The word سَفَرَةٍ comes from the root meaning “to unveil” or “to expose.” These are the angels responsible for unveiling this treasure to humanity. There are two primary interpretations of who these safarah are.

The first opinion, held by Ibn Abbas (RA) and others, is that these are the scribes among the angels—those whose job is to write. All the previous scriptures sent by Allah ﷻ—the Torah, the Injeel, the Psalms of David (AS), the scriptures given to Ibrahim (AS) and Musa (AS)—all of these exist in one grand heavenly book with Allah ﷻ. When Jibreel (AS) is commanded to deliver revelation to earth, certain angels take the original manuscript and transcribe the exact surah or ayat that is to be revealed. They make a certified copy, which is then given to Jibreel (AS) for delivery to the Prophet ﷺ.

The word safarah also means “ambassadors.” These angels are the trusted ambassadors from Allah ﷻ to His messengers, and those messengers then become ambassadors to humanity. The core meaning returns to unveiling—writing unveils knowledge, an ambassador unveils a message from the king to the people, and travel unveils new lands and territories.

The phrase بِأَيْدِى indicates physical possession—“in the hands of.” This is sacred, high-profile access. Like a museum curator who must wear special gloves and maintain specific conditions to handle an ancient manuscript, only these honored angels have access to touch the original.

This is the meaning of being in the hands of those who unveil: otherwise, this revelation would have remained veiled from humanity forever. The original location is described elsewhere as كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ (a well-guarded book), hidden away like treasure.

Ayah 16

كِرَامٍۭ بَرَرَةٍ

Noble and dutiful.

The angels who deliver this revelation are described with two qualities: كِرَامٍ meaning “noble” or “generous,” and بَرَرَةٍ meaning “righteous” or “dutiful.”

This description extends to Jibreel (AS) himself, who is elsewhere in the Quran described as رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (a noble messenger) who is مُطَاعٍ (obeyed) in the heavens. Jibreel (AS) does not descend alone when bringing the Quran. He has a legion following him, and they too are kiram (noble) and bararah (righteous).

There’s a linguistic distinction worth noting. In Arabic, there are two words for the righteous: أَبْرَار and بَرَرَة. Abrar is used for people, while bararah is used for angels. Abrar implies a few good ones, while bararah suggests many good ones. Angels possess far more consistent goodness than humans do. Essentially all angels are good, but not all people are good. So the angels receive the greater plural form.

Allah ﷻ is painting the entire picture of revelation’s descent. A dignified entourage of noble, righteous angels descends from the heavens to honor humanity with this message. This is not just information being transmitted—this is a grand, sacred delivery from the highest realm.

This passage is personally significant because it establishes the attitude with which one should study the Quran. We’re dealing with something very high, very noble, and very sacred. This is what sets the tone for serious engagement with Allah’s word. The Quran should be pondered over, reflected upon, taught, and treated like the treasure it is.

The great tragedy of our Ummah occurs when the Quran is reduced to mere recitation, when memorization happens without understanding the value of what is being memorized. When Quran becomes only about completing a khatm, or recitation at funerals, or decorative calligraphy on walls in rooms where sins are committed, or a protective amulet hanging from a rearview mirror—this is the ultimate disservice to Allah’s Book.

The people before us wrote Quran on bones, on rocks, on animal skin. If we saw that today, we might say, “What disrespect!” But the Quran’s dignity in this world is not on paper or on the top shelf. Its dignity is meant to reside inside the hearts of people. Its purpose is to help them reflect and stand in awe of Allah ﷻ.

One of the most frightening hadith addresses this. The Prophet ﷺ said that a sign of the Day of Judgment is when knowledge disappears. When a companion asked how knowledge could disappear when “we recite Quran, our children will recite it, and their children will recite it,” the Prophet ﷺ responded by pointing to the Christians and Jews who have the Torah and Injeel in their hands, reciting them, yet benefiting nothing from those books. Ibn Abbas (RA) later criticized those who are completely happy with recitation happening while having no understanding of what’s being said.

If Ibn Abbas (RA) were alive today, who would he be talking about? Every Ramadan—the month of Quran—the imam stands and recites beautifully, while the vast majority standing behind him have no idea what he’s saying. They’re happy with the recitation happening even though they have no clue what’s inside it.

This doesn’t make tarawih wrong or salah wrong. It means we need to make up for that gap. We need study of the Quran alongside our recitation. Reciting a few rak’at after knowing what’s being said means you stand before Allah ﷻ understanding His word. That’s why this word came in the first place.

Ayah 17

قُتِلَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَآ أَكْفَرَهُۥ

Destroyed is man! How ungrateful he is!

After describing the grandeur of revelation, Allah ﷻ now addresses a stark question: Who do you think you are? The phrase قُتِلَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ literally means “May the human being be killed” or “Cursed is the human being.” It’s an expression of intense disapproval.

The phrase مَآ أَكْفَرَهُۥ has two meanings. The first is “How incredibly ungrateful can he be?” The second is “How amazing is his level of disbelief and disregard?” Both meanings work together—expressing both the depth of human ingratitude and the shocking extent of human arrogance.

This rhetorical question challenges those who act as though they don’t need revelation. You come from noble tribes, you have dignity and heritage, you were raised in positions of status—but what were you really made from? This is where Allah ﷻ begins a humbling reminder of human origins.

Ayah 18

مِنْ أَىِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقَهُۥ

From what thing did He create him?

This is sarcasm in the Quran. Allah ﷻ obviously knows what He created humans from—He doesn’t need to ask. But the question is posed to make the arrogant person stop and think. You act so elite, you carry yourself with such pride—remind yourself again: from what thing were you created?

The phrase مِنْ أَىِّ شَىْءٍ emphasizes “what thing”—not even “what substance” or “what noble material,” but simply “what thing.” This diminishes any sense of inherent superiority. Whatever status you’ve achieved, whatever position you hold, go back to your origin.

Ayah 19

مِن نُّطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُۥ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ

From a sperm-drop He created him and destined him.

The answer comes bluntly: مِن نُّطْفَةٍ—from a drop of sperm. This is uncomfortable to read, and that discomfort is intentional. Allah ﷻ is describing the graphic reality of the sexual act. Why? Because He’s reminding those who treat His revelation with arrogance: Who do you think you are? You come from something so humble that when it falls on a sheet, people wash it away in embarrassment and consider it filth.

There is nothing more humbling than this origin. Allah ﷻ created the human being from humiliation, from absolute lowliness. This is your starting point—no one is above this reality, regardless of lineage or wealth.

Then خَلَقَهُۥ—He created him. And فَقَدَّرَهُۥ—then He proportioned him, destined him, calculated his development precisely. The word قَدَر means precise calculation, unlike حَرْص, which is wild guessing. Allah ﷻ precisely calculated how many bones you would have, which limbs would function and how well, how far apart your eyes would be, what color they would be, where you would live, where you would die.

Every physical detail of your creation was manufactured inside your mother’s womb according to divine precision. You had no hand in any of it. You didn’t choose your skin color, hair color, eye color, height, or bone structure. This development—this taqdeer—occurred while you were utterly powerless.

Ayah 20

ثُمَّ ٱلسَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُۥ

Then He eased the way for him.

The word ثُمَّ means “then,” indicating sequence. After the precise development inside the womb comes the next stage: ٱلسَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُۥ —He made the path easy for him, He facilitated the way.

This refers to the baby’s passage out of the mother’s body. Allah ﷻ opened the roadway and facilitated it. If Allah ﷻ had not opened that door, you would have died inside your mother. This is your origin, your first home. You were completely dependent, completely powerless.

These are among the most powerful words describing human powerlessness. The journey begins in total dependence and vulnerability. No matter what education you later receive, what capabilities you develop, what status you achieve—before any of that, you came from this place of absolute weakness.

Ayah 21

ثُمَّ أَمَاتَهُۥ فَأَقْبَرَهُۥ

Then He causes him to die and provides a grave for him.

Allah ﷻ fast-forwards past the entire lifespan. You go from barely being alive, emerging from your mother in complete powerlessness, and the very next thing mentioned is ثُمَّ أَمَاتَهُۥ—then He gives you death.

Before you know it, your life is over. Everything in between—education, achievements, status, wealth—none of it is even mentioned. The focus remains on your powerlessness at the beginning and your powerlessness at the end.

Then فَأَقْبَرَهُۥ—He buries him, He places him in a grave. You came out of one belly and entered another. The word قَبْر shares the same root as the womb imagery. The earth becomes your new mother, hugging you again. Earlier in Surah An-Naba’, Allah ﷻ called the earth a cradle (مِهَادًا). Now it embraces you once more.

There’s an anthropological reason many societies speak of “Mother Earth.” You return to her embrace in the end.

Ayah 22

ثُمَّ إِذَا شَآءَ أَنشَرَهُۥ

Then when He wills, He will resurrect him.

After death and burial comes the final stage: ثُمَّ إِذَا شَآءَ—then when He wills, whenever He decides. The timing is entirely in Allah’s hands.

أَنشَرَهُۥ—He will spread him out again, resurrect him, cause him to sprout up again like a plant emerging from the earth. The same earth that received your body will release it again at Allah’s command.

This is the complete life of a human being in fast-forward: from a drop, to development in the womb, to emergence into the world, to death, into the ground, and resurrection. The time between being a baby and dying is so brief it’s barely worth mentioning in this cosmic perspective.

Ayah 23

كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ مَآ أَمَرَهُۥ

No! He has not yet accomplished what He commanded him.

The word كَلَّا appears again—“No!” or “Not at all!” This is a rejection of human pride and a statement of reality.

لَمَّا يَقْضِ—he has not yet fulfilled, he hasn’t finished. The word قَضَى means to be completely done with a task, like finishing a homework assignment or term paper. You’re finished with it, completed it entirely.

But Allah ﷻ says: He hasn’t finished yet. What hasn’t he finished? مَآ أَمَرَهُۥ—what He commanded him. This means responding to revelation, becoming humbled before Allah ﷻ, fulfilling the purpose for which he was created.

The urgency is profound. In those few seconds between birth and death—that brief window of life—you still haven’t done what you were supposed to do. The interim period isn’t even worth detailed mention because it’s so short in the grand scheme, and yet within that small space, the commanded task remains incomplete.

This creates a sense of urgency about our purpose. We’re running out of time to fulfill what Allah ﷻ has commanded.

Ayah 24

فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِۦٓ

Then let mankind look at his food.

After reminding humans of their origin—things too far in the past to remember—and their future death and resurrection—things they cannot yet see—Allah ﷻ now directs attention to something observable in the present moment.

فَلْيَنظُرِ—then let him look, let him take a close look, let him observe carefully. ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِۦٓ—the human being at his food, at the meal he’s about to eat.

The connection to the previous ayah is important. The word أَنَّا at the beginning of the next ayah indicates continuation—this isn’t a new subject but connected to what came before. When you sit with a plate of food in front of you, Allah ﷻ wants you to understand the journey that food took to reach you.

This is a reminder you can access right now. You can’t remember being a drop in your mother’s womb. You can’t see yet what your death will look like. But you can look at your food and trace its journey back to its origin. In doing so, you’ll relive the journey of your own life.

Ayah 25

أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا ٱلْمَآءَ صَبًّا

We poured down water in torrents.

The journey begins with rain. أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا—We poured down, We made pour heavily. The word صَبَّ implies intense, heavy rainfall, not a light drizzle.

This plate of food before you—whether a burger and fries or rice and vegetables—its journey started somewhere with intense rain pouring down. Perhaps in farms in Brazil, or fields in Southeast Asia, or orchards in California. The rain came first.

Ayah 26

ثُمَّ شَقَقْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ شَقًّا

Then We broke open the earth, splitting it.

After the rain came down, ثُمَّ شَقَقْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ—then We tore the earth open, We split it. The intensive form شَقًّا emphasizes the dramatic action. By means of that rain, Allah ﷻ caused the earth to crack open.

This mirrors the earlier description of birth: just as a passage opened for the baby to emerge from the mother, now a passage opens in the earth for the seed to emerge. The parallel is deliberate—one drop impregnates the woman, another drop (of rain) impregnates the earth. Both require an opening for life to come forth.

Ayah 27

فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا حَبًّا

And caused to grow therein grain.

The result of the rain and the splitting of the earth: فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا حَبًّا—We caused seeds to sprout, We brought forth grain. The seed that was buried in darkness now emerges into light, growing into sustenance.

The word حَبّ refers to grain and seeds—the fundamental basis of human food. Every plant that grows is a product of a seed. Everything Allah ﷻ mentions after this is a byproduct of the seed already mentioned.

Ayah 28

وَعِنَبًا وَقَضْبًا

And grapes and herbage.

Now Allah ﷻ begins to specify types of vegetation. وَعِنَبًا —and grapes. This represents sweet fruits, exotic fruits, expensive fruits. Grapes are delicate plants, difficult to grow, and wine is made from them, making them valuable and luxurious.

وَقَضْبًا—and herbage, vegetables, greenery. This refers to plants whose tops are eaten by animals while humans pull out and eat what grows beneath the ground—carrots, potatoes, turnips.

Notice the pairing: grapes versus vegetables. Sweet versus savory. Exotic versus necessary. Expensive versus cheap. Luxury versus staple. Something that can be misused (grapes can become wine) versus something straightforward in its benefit.

Allah ﷻ is showing that the provisions He gives serve multiple functions. One provides luxury and added pleasure; the other provides necessity without which humanity cannot function.

Ayah 29

وَزَيْتُونًا وَنَخْلًا

And olive and palm trees.

The second pairing: وَزَيْتُونًا—and olives. For Arabs, olive oil is the most important oil. It’s the enhancement to all their meals, a digestive aid, used for medicinal purposes. It elevates food, making it taste richer and more complete.

وَنَخْلًا—and palm trees. Notice Allah ﷻ didn’t just mention the date; He mentioned the entire palm tree. Every part of the palm tree is useful: its dates are eaten, its seeds are crushed for flour or oil, its bark is used for construction, its leaves are woven into clothing, mats, and shelters, even weapons are made from it.

This pairing represents enhancement versus comprehensive necessity. Olive oil enhances meals and health; the palm tree provides complete, multi-purpose sustenance and utility.

Ayah 30

وَحَدَآئِقَ غُلْبًا

And gardens of dense shrubbery.

Now comes the culmination: وَحَدَآئِقَ غُلْبًا—and gardens that are overwhelming, gardens so thick with growth that they envelope you. The word غُلْب suggests gardens so dense and lush that when you’re inside them, you’re overwhelmed by their greenery. You’re in a different world entirely.

This represents the most beautiful manifestation of a seed—when it evolves into a complete garden. The ultimate expression of that original seed is the garden, where everything comes together in harmony.

Ayah 31

وَفَـٰكِهَةً وَأَبًّا

And fruit and grass.

Inside these gardens, you find وَفَـٰكِهَةً—fruits. The word فَاكِهَة comes from فَكَاهَة, meaning something that brings joy when you bite into it, something that makes you smile. Like biting into a perfect strawberry or a juicy orange—you can’t help but smile.

وَأَبًّا—and grass, soft vegetation you walk upon. When do fruits and grass come together naturally? When you’re walking through a garden, stepping on soft grass to reach the trees bearing fruit.

This isn’t a random list. Allah ﷻ is painting a complete picture of how provisions beautify life.

Ayah 32

مَّتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ

As provision for you and your grazing livestock.

The purpose of all this is stated: مَّتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ —as enjoyment for you and for your cattle, as sustenance for you and your animals.

This phrase recalls Surah An-Naba’ where the same pairing appeared. It serves multiple functions here. First, it should invoke gratitude—this elaborate journey from rain to earth to growth to harvest exists for your benefit.

Second, it humbles you by placing you alongside your animals. The surah began with قُتِلَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَآ أَكْفَرَهُۥ (Destroyed is man, how ungrateful he is!). It ends this section by saying: I feed you like I feed your animals. This is humbling because the passage began with human arrogance.

Through your own beginning, you should learn humility. Through the food’s journey, you should learn gratitude.

There’s also a structural beauty here. Allah ﷻ first mentioned one item: the seed (حَبًّا). Then two items per ayah: grapes and vegetables, olives and palm trees. Then back to one item: gardens. Then one item again: fruits (with grass). The pairing emphasizes that provisions serve dual purposes—necessity and beauty, sustenance and pleasure.

When Allah ﷻ provides, He doesn’t just give us nutrients. He packages them beautifully. We don’t need a grape to taste delicious or have brilliant color—we just need its sugars and vitamins. But Allah ﷻ wraps nutrition in beauty. This is the sunnah of Allah ﷻ in creation: it serves a purpose and adds beauty.

The revelation Allah ﷻ gave us functions the same way. It should serve two functions: our survival (spiritual purpose) and our appreciation of beauty. We must study the Quran exploring its beauty, and we must study it to find our purpose. Both are necessary.

This is why proper recitation matters. When you recite with tajweed, you slow down, pronounce every letter properly, giving yourself opportunity to reflect—that’s the purpose of clear recitation. When you recite melodiously, the rhythm synchronizes with your heart while the words engage your mind. The Quran came to conquer both hearts and minds, and everything in our deen is connected to that purpose.

The word أَنْعَام specifically refers to calm, docile cattle—animals with dulled reflexes. Unlike cats or birds or even squirrels that react quickly to danger, cattle are slow and unresponsive. A cow standing by a busy highway doesn’t even flinch as cars rush past.

This detail becomes significant in the next passage.

Ayah 33

فَإِذَا جَآءَتِ ٱلصَّآخَّةُ

But when there comes the Deafening Blast.

The scene shifts dramatically. فَإِذَا جَآءَتِ ٱلصَّآخَّةُ—when the deafening scream comes, when the loud blast arrives that overwhelms and deafens.

The word الصَّاخَّة describes a sound so intense it deafens you, like in films when a bomb explodes nearby and the character wakes up unable to hear properly—everything is muffled, people are screaming but you can’t hear them. That overwhelming, deafening quality is as-sakhkhah.

Notice the connection: cattle were just mentioned—calm, unreactive animals. But when a loud explosion happens, even cattle run in terror. The implication is devastating: on that Day, you will be no different from animals. You’ll panic and flee like frightened livestock.

Ayah 34

يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ ٱلْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ

The Day a man will flee from his brother.

يَوْمَ —the Day. يَفِرُّ —he will flee. The word فِرَار specifically means running with terror, like an animal fleeing a predator. If a dog is chasing you, you do firar. If someone is running from the police, they’re doing firar. This isn’t casual running—it’s panicked escape.

ٱلْمَرْءُ —a person, but more specifically, someone with مُرُوءَة, meaning chivalry, dignity, nobility, someone who cares for others. This is the person who helps the elderly cross the street, who looks out for the vulnerable. That’s a mar’.

مِنْ أَخِيهِ—from his brother. In Arabic, أَخ doesn’t only mean sibling; it also means friend, companion, partner. There’s a famous Arabic saying: “Whoever looks for a brother without flaws will be left without a brother”—meaning if you seek perfect friends, you’ll have no friends.

So this person of dignity and care will run in terror from his own sibling or closest friend. Why? Why would you be terrified of your brother or friend? Perhaps because you wronged them, or perhaps because they genuinely like you and say, “Hey, you’re my friend—can I have some of your good deeds?” And you respond, “Get away from me!”

Ayah 35

وَأُمِّهِۦ وَأَبِيهِ

And his mother and his father.

وَأُمِّهِۦ وَأَبِيهِ—and his mother and his father. He’ll run from his parents for the same reason. Perhaps he wasn’t good to his mother, and now she says, “Come here, give me your good deeds. Remember when you yelled at me? I need your hasanat. I know you went to Hajj, but those rewards are mine now.” Or maybe he was good to them, but they still want his good deeds, and he thinks, “No, don’t take my stuff!” Either way, he runs from his own mother and father.

Ayah 36

وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ وَبَنِيهِ

And his wife and his children.

وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ—and his companion. Allah ﷻ didn’t say زَوْجَتِهِ (his wife) or اِمْرَأَتِهِ (his woman). He said صَاحِبَتِهِ—his companion, the woman he was in love with, the one he wanted to be with all the time, the one he missed when apart from her, the one he kept texting, “I miss you, I miss your smile, I can’t wait to see you again.”

That level of companionship and affection—that’s sahibah. And he’s running from her.

وَبَنِيهِ —and his children. Notice the pairings again. First, his friend (one relationship). Then two together: mother and father. Then two more: wife and children.

In a man’s life, there are three basic circles: you’re either with your friends, with your parents, or with your wife and kids. When you’re not with friends, you’re in one of the other two. When parents become too much, you go to the wife and kids. When wife and kids drive you crazy, you go to your parents. When both are overwhelming, you escape to your friends to find sanity. Then the wife complains, “Why are you always with your friends?” Then the parents say, “Your wife just owns you—you never visit us anymore.”

You ping-pong between these three circles trying to find balance. This is life. These three circles preoccupy us. Even when making a living, for whom are you working? Most often, not for yourself—for the wife, the kids, the parents. Of all these relationships, the most selfish and self-serving time is with friends, because there are no obligations there—just companionship and complaining about your obligations elsewhere.

Allah ﷻ began with the most selfish circle (friends), moved to the strongest bond that’s been with you since birth (parents), then to the bond developed later that came with both joy and responsibility (spouse and children). These three circles preoccupy us completely.

But on that Day, you have a different preoccupation.

Ayah 37

لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ

For every one of them that Day will be a matter adequate for him.

لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ —for every single person among them. يَوْمَئِذٍ —on that Day. شَأْنٌ —a concern, a matter, something on their mind.

The word شَأْن refers to something that only you can worry about, something that concerns you alone and cannot concern anybody else. It’s the same word used for Allah ﷻ Himself: كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ (Every day He is in a matter)—because there are things only Allah ﷻ can do that nobody else can do. Rain coming from the sky is only Allah’s concern. The sun’s fuel is only Allah’s concern.

That Day, you will have a concern that is exclusively yours, that nobody else shares or cares about except you.

يُغْنِيهِ —it will make him independent, free him from all other concerns. غِنَى means to be in no need of anyone or anything else. You will not need anyone. You will not care about anyone.

Mother? Don’t care. Father? Don’t care. Friend? What friend? Wife? Child? Who cares? Complete detachment. You’re freed from all other worries because this one concern consumes you entirely.

Ayah 38

وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّسْفِرَةٌ

Some faces, that Day, will be bright.

Now comes the final scene—two groups on Judgment Day. وُجُوهٌ —faces. يَوْمَئِذٍ —on that Day. مُّسْفِرَةٌ —radiant, lit up, shining bright.

The word أَسْفَرَ describes when the morning becomes extra bright. Some faces on that Day will be truly illuminated with light.

Ayah 39

ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ

Laughing, rejoicing.

ضَاحِكَةٌ —laughing, unable to contain their joy. مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ —receiving good news, feeling congratulated and welcomed.

The word مُسْتَبْشِرَة comes from بِشْر, which means skin. When skin is illuminated and radiant, when someone looks at you and says, “You’re glowing!”—that’s istibshar. Like when you receive a text that you’re valedictorian and light seems to come from your face and people ask, “What happened to you?”—that’s mustabshirah.

Why are they laughing? Why are their faces lit up? When does someone’s face light up? When they see something. The surah began with someone who was blind. Now, those who weren’t spiritually blind in this life—their eyes are opened on Judgment Day and they see Allah ﷻ. And Allah ﷻ is نُورُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (the Light of the heavens and the earth).

Some faces are full of light because they’re in the presence of Light. They’re in the presence of Allah ﷻ. The light in their chest, in their hands, illuminates their faces. Their faces are radiant.

This picture is completed in another surah where Allah ﷻ says: وَأَنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ٱلْمُنتَهَىٰ - وَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ أَضْحَكَ وَأَبْكَىٰ—He is the One to whom there is the final return, and He is the One who makes you laugh and makes you cry. Judgment Day is special because when you see Allah ﷻ, you will laugh with joy and cry with joy simultaneously.

Here, Allah ﷻ says they’re laughing out loud, unable to contain the happiness of being in His company, feeling His congratulations and welcome.

Ayah 40

وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ

And some faces, that Day, will have upon them dust.

The contrast: وَوُجُوهٌ —and other faces. يَوْمَئِذٍ —on that Day. عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ —upon them will be dust, dirt piled on them, covered in dust.

When the face is covered in dust, the eyes cannot see properly. Visibility is blocked. What’s in front of you? Dirt. Just dirt.

Ayah 41

تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ

Blackness will cover them.

تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ—overwhelming them will be qatarah. This is unique vocabulary in the Quran.

The word رَهَق means to overwhelm or cover someone. قَتَر has several meanings in Arabic: stupidity or foolishness; being inclined toward evil; being under accusation or allegation; and black smoke or smut that colors and darkens whatever it touches.

When someone is bankrupt—completely broke and homeless—you can see it in the blackness and exhaustion on their face. That darkness, that visible mark of being utterly defeated—that’s qatarah.

These faces are covered in black smoke. Some scholars say this smoke comes from the Hellfire already beginning to envelope them. Others say Allah ﷻ does not want to see them, and they are not permitted to see Him, so they’re surrounded by dark smoke—because in other places in the Quran, Allah ﷻ says He won’t even look at them.

Beyond the literal smoke, there’s also the reality that depression, guilt, and despair physically change your complexion. You become pale, darkened, with bags under your eyes. That darkness overshadows you. This is the imagery: physical black smoke and the spiritual darkness of being rejected and lost.

Ayah 42

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَفَرَةُ ٱلْفَجَرَةُ

Those are the disbelievers, the wicked ones.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ—those, in fact. هُمُ—they are. ٱلْكَفَرَةُ—the disbelievers, those who covered the truth. ٱلْفَجَرَةُ—the wicked, those who exploded into sin.

Earlier, Allah ﷻ said وُجُوهٌ—some faces—without specifying who they are. Now He specifies: those dark faces belong to the disbelievers and the wicked.

The word فَجَرَة comes from فَجْر, which literally means explosion—like how light explodes at dawn (that’s why the morning prayer is called Fajr). A fajir is someone who explodes into whatever behavior they want, saying whatever words come to their mouth, with no concern for consequences. They do whatever they want whenever they want.

That’s the simple definition of a fajir: someone who lives without restraint, without accountability, without concern for anything beyond their immediate desires.

The Structure of Surah ’Abasa

Now that we’ve reached the end of the surah, the most remarkable aspect is how it’s organized. The surah has six parts, which can be understood in three pairs.

The First Pair: Attitudes Toward Revelation (Ayahs 1-16)

The first passage showed two attitudes toward revelation: the blind man who eagerly sought to learn, and the elite person who felt he didn’t need anything. One person specified (the blind one), the other left unnamed (made into a nobody). Allah ﷻ reversed their status.

The second passage described the grand, noble reality of revelation—how it’s elevated (مَّرْفُوعَةٍ), purified (مُّطَهَّرَةٍ), in the hands of noble angels (سَفَرَةٍ كِرَامٍۭ بَرَرَةٍ).

These two passages work together: How dare you have the wrong attitude toward revelation when it comes from such a high origin? This establishes both the status of revelation and the right attitude that should accompany it.

The Middle Pair: Two Journeys (Ayahs 17-32)

The third passage described the journey of the human being: from a drop, to development in the womb, to birth, to death, to burial, to resurrection.

The fourth passage described the journey of food: from rain, to the earth splitting open, to seeds sprouting, to plants growing, to fruits ripening, to harvest.

Notice: both begin with a drop. One drop impregnates the woman; another drop (rain) impregnates the earth. Both involve an opening—the womb opens for the child to emerge; the earth splits open for the seed to emerge. In both, Allah ﷻ places something back in the ground—the dead body returns to earth; the seed is planted in earth. From both comes forth life again.

If you can’t remember your own journey through life, Allah ﷻ makes you relive it through the food you eat. Every meal has traveled through a profound journey—rain from thousands of miles away, soil prepared over months, crops tended, harvested, shipped, processed, cooked, delivered—all before reaching your plate. You went through an equally profound journey to be sitting here. Neither journey is meaningless; both have a destination ahead.

These two middle journeys teach humility and gratitude. Your beginning should make you humble. The food’s journey should make you grateful.

The Final Pair: Two Scenes of Judgment Day (Ayahs 33-42)

The fifth passage showed the loud scream (الصَّآخَّةُ) when everyone runs away from those they love—friend, parents, spouse, children. Everyone is preoccupied with their own concern (شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ).

The sixth passage showed two types of faces: some radiant, laughing, rejoicing (مُّسْفِرَةٌ ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ); others covered in dust and black smoke (غَبَرَةٌ… قَتَرَةٌ).

These final passages contrast with the opening. In the beginning, the elite person felt like nothing preoccupied them (اسْتَغْنَىٰ). Now something definitely preoccupies them (شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ). The root is the same word—you acted like you needed nothing; now you’re desperately preoccupied.

In the opening, the blind man ran toward truth. In the closing, those who didn’t run toward truth now run away from everyone they love.

The purified revelation (مُّطَهَّرَةٍ) handled by angels became the source of purified faces (مُّسْفِرَةٌ) for the believers—their faces lit like the angels are lit when they touch revelation.

Allah ﷻ kept the arrogant person anonymous in the opening (مَنِ اسْتَغْنَىٰ), but specified him at the end: أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَفَرَةُ ٱلْفَجَرَةُ (Those are the disbelievers, the wicked ones). The mystery is solved.

The Middle Passages and the Mention of Cattle

An interesting detail: Allah ﷻ mentioned sustenance for “you and your cattle” (لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ). Cattle (أَنْعَام) are the calmest, most docile animals—no reflexes, no reactions. A cow standing by a highway doesn’t flinch as cars rush past.

But when the loud explosion happens (الصَّآخَّةُ), even cattle run in panic. The implication: on Judgment Day, you will be no different from animals in your terror. This is Allah’s way of saying there are only two treatments on that Day—you’ll be treated like a human being, or you’ll be treated like an animal.

Two Types of Sustenance

There are two types of sustenance in this surah: the sustenance of revelation, and the sustenance of food. Food is for both humans and animals. Revelation is only for humans.

Some people in this life concern themselves only with sustenance they share with animals. They don’t concern themselves with what makes them superior to animals—feeding their soul with revelation. What should we expect on Judgment Day? They’ll be herded around, running crazy, like animals.

Those who purify and cleanse themselves in this life through revelation will have purified faces in the next. The blind one who was poor, who tripped and fell with dirt on his face in this life—his face will be full of light in the next life. The one who was elite, above the dirt and smog, will be covered in dirt and black smoke. The equation will be completely flipped.

That is the beautiful contrast and profound imagery of this surah. May Allah ﷻ help us appreciate the power and beauty of His Quran.

Ayah 1–10 · linked video: 01. ’Abasa (Ayah 1-10)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

عَبَسَ (’abasa) [ع-ب-س]

The root ع-ب-س carries the meaning of frowning or showing displeasure on one’s face. The verb ’abasa specifically refers to the initial, slight bulge that appears between the eyebrows when one begins to frown - not a full scowl but the very first indication of frustration. This is distinct from عَبَسَ with a seen, which would indicate more visible bulging where teeth might show, and from بَسَرَ which indicates inappropriate behavior accompanying the frown. The choice of ’abasa here emphasizes the minimal nature of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم reaction - merely a slight furrow of concern, not anger or rudeness.

تَوَلَّىٰ (tawalla) [و-ل-ي]

The root و-ل-ي relates to turning toward or away from something. The Form V verb tawalla means to turn oneself away deliberately. What makes this word choice remarkable is that the Quran describes the scene not as “he turned toward the leaders” but as “he turned away from” the blind man. This reverses the expected perspective - normally one would describe turning toward the more important person, but Allah جل جلاله flips the frame to show that turning away from the blind companion was what mattered, establishing who the real VIP was in Allah’s sight.

Ayah 2

الْأَعْمَىٰ (al-a’ma) [ع-م-ي]

The root ع-م-ي means blindness. Rather than using Abdullah’s name, Allah describes him as “the blind one.” Far from being degrading, this highlights the extra effort required of someone with visual impairment to seek out the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم. The definite article “al” makes this descriptive - “the blind one” - emphasizing his condition as something that makes his arrival more remarkable, not less. This word choice embeds recognition of the additional struggle faced by those with disabilities in pursuing religious knowledge.

جَاءَ (ja’a) [ج-ي-ء]

The root ج-ي-ء means to come or arrive. The verb ja’a indicates he came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, but later ayahs will use stronger verbs (yas’a - came running/hastening) to emphasize the effort involved. Even this basic verb “came” acknowledges action and initiative on the part of Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه.

Ayah 3

يُدْرِيكَ (yudrika) [د-ر-ي]

The root د-ر-ي means to know, perceive, or have insight into something. The Form IV verb yudri means to make someone know or to give someone the ability to perceive. The rhetorical question “What would make you know?” (ma yudrika) implies that there is no way for you to know - no clue, no indication available to discover what is truly in this person’s heart. This emphasizes that outward behavior, especially when it seems socially inappropriate, does not reveal inner spiritual reality.

يَزَّكَّىٰ (yazzakka) [ز-ك-ي]

The root ز-ك-ي means to purify, cleanse, or grow. The Form V verb yazzakka (with doubled middle radical) means to purify oneself, to seek self-purification. The choice of Form V emphasizes the person’s own effort and initiative in seeking purification. The word is in the imperfect tense, suggesting ongoing or potential action - “perhaps he seeks to purify himself,” indicating possibility and hope rather than certainty.

Ayah 4

يَذَّكَّرُ (yadhdhakkaru) [ذ-ك-ر]

The root ذ-ك-ر means to remember or mention. The Form V verb yadhdhakkaru means to take admonition, to reflect and remember deeply. This is different from simple remembering - it implies taking something to heart, pondering it, letting it settle in the mind and heart. The Form V indicates intensive, deliberate effort to remember and benefit from what is heard.

الذِّكْرَىٰ (al-dhikra) [ذ-ك-ر]

From the same root ذ-ك-ر, al-dhikra is the noun meaning “the reminder” or “the remembrance.” This refers to the beneficial words of guidance being shared. The definite article emphasizes a specific, powerful reminder - not just any words, but meaningful guidance that carries lasting benefit.

تَنفَعُهُ (tanfa’uhu) [ن-ف-ع]

The root ن-ف-ع means to benefit or profit. The verb tanfa’u means to benefit, and the attached pronoun “hu” means “him.” The phrasing “so the reminder would benefit him” acknowledges that benefit may not be immediate - a person might hear something and only benefit from it later, perhaps years down the road, when circumstances change and those words suddenly become relevant and applicable.

Ayah 5

اسْتَغْنَىٰ (istaghna) [غ-ن-ي]

The root غ-ن-ي means wealth, being free from need, self-sufficiency. The Form X verb istaghna means to consider oneself self-sufficient, to feel no need for something or someone. This is the attitude of someone who believes they have everything they need - materially, emotionally - and therefore sees no need for spiritual guidance. The Form X emphasizes the subjective perception: “he considers himself” or “he feels himself” to be without need, whether or not this is objectively true.

Ayah 6

تَصَدَّىٰ (tasadda) [ص-د-ي]

The root ص-د-ي relates to echoing sounds and relentless pursuit. The verb tasadda means to give attention to someone persistently, to pursue their attention relentlessly. It comes from the imagery of a bird (asada) that chirps relentlessly at night, jumping from branch to branch, never quiet. It also relates to sada, the echo in a valley that keeps coming back. The Form V verb tasadda captures the idea of going back again and again, pursuing someone’s attention with determination, like an echo that won’t stop returning.

Ayah 7

عَلَيْكَ (alayka) [ع-ل-ي]

The preposition ’ala with the attached pronoun ka means “upon you” or “incumbent upon you.” Here it carries a tone of burden or responsibility: “What is it upon you…?” The phrasing questions why the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم should bear responsibility or concern for someone’s purification when that person has no interest in it.

يَزَّكَّىٰ (yazzakka) [ز-ك-ي]

The same verb appears again from Ayah 3, but here in a negative construction: “that he does not purify himself.” The shift from “perhaps he seeks to purify himself” (about the blind man) to “that he does not purify himself” (about the wealthy leader) is stark. Allah جل جلاله is not saying “if he doesn’t purify himself” but rather declaring “he will not purify himself” - a definitive statement about the spiritual reality Allah knows but which is hidden from human perception.

Ayah 8

جَاءَكَ (ja’aka) [ج-ي-ء]

The same root ج-ي-ء meaning to come, but now with the attached pronoun “you” (ka). The verb “came to you” is simple and direct, but what follows builds on this basic arrival with additional descriptions of effort and spiritual state.

يَسْعَىٰ (yas’a) [س-ع-ي]

The root س-ع-ي means to hasten, to strive, to exert effort in moving toward something. The verb yas’a means he came hastening, running, making effort. This is stronger than just “came” (ja’a) - it emphasizes the exertion, especially significant given that this is a blind person navigating to find the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم. The word captures both physical effort and eagerness, enthusiasm to reach the destination.

Ayah 9

يَخْشَىٰ (yakhsha) [خ-ش-ي]

The root خ-ش-ي means to fear with reverence, to have awe and consciousness of Allah. This is different from خ-و-ف (khawf), which is ordinary fear. Khashya is the fear of someone you love and respect, the fear that keeps you conscious of Allah’s presence, the fear that leads to righteousness. The imperfect verb yakhsha means “he fears” - a present, continuous state of being. This is the fear mentioned in other passages: “innama yakhsha Allaha min ’ibadihi al-’ulama” - only those with knowledge truly fear Allah. Here Allah جل جلاله certifies that this blind companion carries this noble quality of khashya in his heart.

Ayah 10

تَلَهَّىٰ (talahha) [ل-ه-ي]

The root ل-ه-ي means to be distracted, diverted, or preoccupied from something. The Form V verb talahha means to allow oneself to be distracted, to turn one’s attention away from something toward something else. Even the slightest distraction - not rudeness, not dismissal, but mere distraction - is what Allah addresses here. The verb choice emphasizes that the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم attention was diverted, even if only slightly and with good intention, from the one who truly deserved it according to Allah’s criteria of value.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

The opening with two past-tense verbs ’abasa and tawalla without a subject creates dramatic immediacy - the actions are thrust before us without identification. Only with “an ja’ahu” (that the blind one came to him) do we understand who “he” is. This grammatical structure mirrors how a witness might describe a scene: first the actions that caught their attention, then the explanation of who and why.

The dual verb structure (’abasa wa tawalla - he frowned and he turned away) creates a sequence: internal reaction followed by external action. The internal frustration manifests in outward turning away.

Ayah 2

The use of “an” (that/because) introduces the reason clause: the frowning and turning happened “because the blind one came.” The definite article with “al-a’ma” (THE blind one) particularizes this person - not just any blind person, but this specific blind companion whose blindness is now a defining characteristic of his honored status.

The absence of Abdullah’s name is rhetorically significant - Allah could have said “an ja’ahu Abdullah” but instead chose “an ja’ahu al-a’ma.” This focuses attention on the condition that makes his arrival more effortful and therefore more valuable.

Ayah 3-4

The rhetorical question “wa ma yudrika” (and what would make you know?) is a classical Arabic device called istifham inkari - a question that implies impossibility. It’s not genuinely asking what would make you know; it’s asserting that there IS nothing that would make you know. This emphasizes human limitation in perceiving spiritual realities.

The double possibility structure “la’allahu yazzakka aw yadhdhakkaru fa tanfa’uhu al-dhikra” uses three verbs in sequence, building layers of potential benefit: (1) perhaps he purifies himself, (2) or he takes reminder, (3) so the reminder benefits him. The conjunction “aw” (or) presents alternatives, while “fa” (so/then) shows consequence. This grammatical structure acknowledges multiple pathways to benefit - immediate transformation or delayed impact.

The shift from Form V (yazzakka - he purifies himself through his own effort) to the noun form (al-dhikra - the reminder) followed by a simple verb (tanfa’u - it benefits) shows the relationship: personal striving meets divine guidance, resulting in benefit.

Ayah 5-7

The parallel structure “amma man” (as for the one who) introduces two contrasting characters. “Amma man istaghna fa anta lahu tasadda” (as for the one who considers himself without need, so you give him attention) is followed in Ayah 8 by “wa amma man ja’aka yas’a” (and as for the one who came to you hastening). This “amma man… wa amma man” structure is a classical rhetorical device for contrast, forcing the listener to compare the two types directly.

The pronoun emphasis in “fa ANTA lahu tasadda” (so YOU - you of all people - give HIM attention) uses word order for emphasis. Normal word order would be “fa tasadda lahu” but placing “anta” (you) first emphasizes the incongruity: YOU, the noble messenger, pursuing HIM.

The rhetorical question “wa ma ’alayka alla yazzakka” can be read two ways depending on how we understand the negative: (1) “What is it upon you that he does not purify himself?” (i.e., you’re not responsible), or (2) “What concern is it of yours, seeing that he will not purify himself?” The second reading contains Allah’s knowledge that this person WON’T purify himself, making the Prophet’s efforts futile.

Ayah 8-10

The beautiful grammatical parallelism continues: “wa amma man ja’aka yas’a wa huwa yakhsha” uses three verbs/participles to build a complete picture: he came (ja’a), he came hastening (yas’a), and he is one who fears (huwa yakhsha). The threefold description elevates this person’s status with each additional clause.

The hal construction “wa huwa yakhsha” (while he is fearing/in a state of fear) indicates a simultaneous state - his coming and his fearing are concurrent, inseparable qualities.

The final rebuke “fa anta ’anhu talahha” (so you from him are distracted) again uses the pronoun “anta” emphatically. The preposition “’an” (from/away from) with the attached pronoun “hu” (him) emphasizes the source of distraction: you are distracted AWAY FROM him, when he should have been the focus.

The word order throughout these ayahs places maximum emphasis on the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم actions and the blind man’s spiritual state, creating a powerful contrast with the wealthy leader who is described only by his attitude of self-sufficiency, with no spiritual qualities mentioned.

Historical & Biblical Context

Historical Context of Revelation

This passage was revealed in the early Makkan period when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم was still struggling to gain a hearing among the Quraysh leadership. The specific incident occurred when he was speaking with some of the wealthy, influential leaders of Quraysh. Various narrations identify these leaders differently - some mention Abdul Uzza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, others Abu Jahl, and some suggest it was a group of elite leaders together. What’s certain is that these were men of significant political and social influence in Makkan society.

For once, these leaders were giving the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم a genuine opportunity to speak with them and they were actually listening - a rare occurrence given their typical antagonism. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم recognized this as a unique opportunity for dawah, understanding that if even one or two influential people accepted Islam, their conversion could open doors for many others due to their social standing.

Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum

The companion who interrupted was Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه, related to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم through marriage connections (through Khadijah رضي الله عنها). He was blind from birth or early childhood. According to various narrations, he approached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم saying either “Ya Rasul Allah, guide me” or “Teach me from what Allah has taught you” or, in one narration found in the books of tafsir, he called out “Ya Muhammad” without using the title “Ya Rasul Allah.”

Importantly, the later revelation commanding believers not to call the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم casually (“la taj’alu du’aa al-rasuli baynakum ka du’a’i ba’dikum ba’da” - don’t call the Prophet like you call one another) had not yet been revealed, so this manner of address, while it would later be inappropriate, was not yet established as improper.

Abdullah’s request was general - not an emergency, not a specific crisis. He simply wanted to learn something beneficial. Given that he was family and that this was early Makkah when there weren’t crowds of companions vying for the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم time, he had regular access to the Messenger. This wasn’t his only chance to learn.

The Prophet’s Response and Revelation

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, caught between two audiences, showed slight frustration (’abasa - a small furrow between the eyebrows) and turned toward the Quraysh leaders, saying something to the effect of “Do you see any problem with what I’ve shared with you so far?” - a gentle attempt to check in with them while not rudely dismissing Abdullah.

At that moment, revelation began to descend. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم had no control over when revelation came - “wa ma yantiqu ’an al-hawa” (nor does he speak from his own desire). He had to recite what was being revealed immediately, even though it was a correction of his action in that very moment, in front of both audiences.

Prophetic Pattern

The Quran acknowledges that previous prophets also focused on reaching influential leaders. Musa عليه السلام was sent first and foremost to Pharaoh (“idhhab ila fir’awn innahu tagha”), then to the rest of the nation. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم was sent to “Umm al-Qura” (the Mother of Towns - Makkah), the central hub of Arabian society, and within it, to those with the most influence.

Earlier prophets like Nuh عليه السلام faced elite classes who demanded special accommodations: “They said, ‘Should we believe in you while the most wretched follow you?’” The elite of Nuh’s people wanted private meetings away from the “riffraff” - the slaves, the poor, the insignificant members of society.

Permanent Impact

After this revelation, the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم treatment of Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه changed permanently. Every time Abdullah would meet him, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم would ask “Is there anything you need? Anything I can do for you?” And Abdullah would often respond, “Marhaban biman ’atabani fihi Rabbi” (Welcome to the one on whose account my Lord corrected me).

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم later appointed Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه as his deputy in charge of Madinah on multiple occasions, demonstrating the elevated status this companion held. He was martyred at the Battle of Qadisiyyah, still serving Islam with distinction.

Coherence

Connection to Surah An-Nazi’at

The immediately preceding surah, An-Nazi’at, concluded with themes directly relevant to this opening passage. An-Nazi’at speaks of the Day when “yatazakkaru al-insanu ma sa’a” - the Day when man will remember all that he strove for. The word sa’a (strove/made effort) appears again in this surah: “wa amma man ja’aka yas’a” (as for one who came to you striving/hastening). The blind companion making effort to reach the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم echoes the theme of human striving that will be remembered on the Day of Judgment.

An-Nazi’at addresses Pharaoh with the call “fa qul hal laka ila an tazakka” (Say: would you purify yourself?) and “wa ahdiyaka ila rabbika fa takhsha” (and I will guide you to your Lord so that you may fear). These same two concepts - purification (tazkiya) and fear (khashya) - appear prominently in Surah Abasa. The hope was that Pharaoh would purify himself and fear Allah; instead, we find these qualities in the blind companion: “la’allahu yazzakka” (perhaps he seeks to purify himself) and “wa huwa yakhsha” (while he fears Allah).

The reversal is deliberate: Pharaoh, one of the most powerful humans who ever lived, lacked these qualities despite being addressed by a messenger. The blind companion, with no worldly status, possessed both. This connection reinforces the Quranic theme that true value lies in spiritual state, not worldly position.

Internal Coherence: The Two Audiences

The passage is structured around two parallel sections, each describing one person:

Section 1 (Ayahs 5-7): The Self-Sufficient One - Description: “amma man istaghna” (as for one who considers himself without need) - The Prophet’s response: “fa anta lahu tasadda” (so you give him attention persistently) - Allah’s verdict: “wa ma ’alayka alla yazzakka” (though you’re not responsible that he won’t purify himself)

Section 2 (Ayahs 8-10): The Fearful One - Description: “wa amma man ja’aka yas’a wa huwa yakhsha” (and as for one who came to you hastening while he fears) - The Prophet’s response: “fa anta ’anhu talahha” (so you from him are distracted) - Implied divine verdict: This one DOES seek purification and DOES fear Allah

The introduction (Ayahs 1-4) frames the incident with both rebuke and mercy: rebuke in describing the frown and turning away, mercy in the double possibility “la’allahu yazzakka aw yadhdhakkaru fa tanfa’uhu al-dhikra” (perhaps he will purify himself, or take reminder so the reminder benefits him).

Progression of Knowledge

The passage moves from external observation to internal spiritual reality:

  1. What anyone could see (Ayahs 1-2): He frowned, he turned away because a blind man came
  2. What humans cannot know (Ayahs 3-4): “What would make you know?” - perhaps internal spiritual realities you cannot perceive
  3. What Allah knows (Ayahs 5-7): This one will NOT purify himself (definitive statement)
  4. What Allah certifies (Ayahs 8-9): This one DOES fear Allah (divine stamp of approval)

This progression teaches that human judgment based on external factors (wealth, status, influence, even outward behavior) cannot perceive what truly matters - the state of the heart known only to Allah.

Thematic Unity

The entire passage revolves around the theme of true value versus perceived value. Every element reinforces this:

  • The “blind one” (seemingly less valuable) is described in terms that emphasize his effort and spiritual state
  • The “self-sufficient one” (seemingly more valuable) is described only by his attitude of independence from Allah
  • The Prophet’s natural human inclination toward the influential is corrected by divine perspective
  • The vocabulary choices (tasadda for pursuing the elite vs. talahha for being distracted from the humble) create moral judgment through word selection
  • The progression from “perhaps” regarding the blind man’s purification to certainty regarding the elite’s lack of purification shows divine knowledge overriding human assumption

Psychological, Political & Social

The Psychology of Excitement and Social Awareness

Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum’s رضي الله عنه interruption reveals a profound psychological truth: when someone is genuinely excited to learn, especially someone who has limited opportunities, they can become blind to social context. This isn’t rudeness - it’s the overwhelming nature of enthusiasm that crowds out awareness of surroundings.

This psychological reality is embedded in the phrase “an ja’ahu al-a’ma” - the blind one came to him. The physical blindness parallels a temporary “social blindness” created by excitement. He heard the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم voice and everything else faded away. He didn’t hear the other voices (which were familiar to him - these Quraysh leaders spoke publicly often) because he was so focused on getting guidance from the Messenger.

This teaches us not to judge people’s intentions by their outward behavior, especially when they may be operating from a different emotional or psychological state than we assume.

The Psychology of the Impaired

Allah’s choice to emphasize “al-a’ma” (the blind one) rather than Abdullah’s name draws attention to a crucial social-psychological reality: those with disabilities must exert extra effort to access what others take for granted.

A blind person navigating to find someone requires: - Heightened auditory awareness - Physical courage to move through space they cannot see - Reliance on others or memory of spatial layouts - Greater time and energy expenditure

By highlighting the blindness, Allah embeds recognition of this extra struggle directly into the Quranic text. This serves multiple purposes:
