  1. Acknowledging invisible effort: Society often fails to recognize the additional energy required by those with impairments to participate in normal activities

  2. Reframing disability: What might seem like a limitation becomes, in Allah’s framing, evidence of greater determination and sincerity

  3. Social isolation factor: The impaired often face social isolation - they don’t play on playgrounds the same way, don’t participate in gatherings as easily, are often made to feel “other.” This isolation can make their behavior seem “different” or “inappropriate” by mainstream social standards

  4. Loudness and assertiveness: People who are frequently dismissed or ignored often develop louder, more assertive communication styles just to be acknowledged. What might seem like social inappropriateness is actually a learned response to being treated as invisible

Class Hierarchy and Social Dynamics

The passage directly confronts social hierarchies that exist in every human society:

Political Elite vs. Ordinary People

The Quraysh leaders represented wealth, political power, and social influence. In any society, these people set trends, make decisions that affect others, control resources, and shape public opinion. The natural human inclination - even among prophets - is to prioritize access to such people because:

  • Their conversion could influence many others
  • They control gatekeepers to broader social acceptance
  • They have platforms to amplify messages
  • Rejection by them can mean widespread rejection

This is why previous prophets also approached their societies’ elite. Musa عليه السلام went first to Pharaoh. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم was sent to “Umm al-Qura” - the central hub of power and influence.

The Dangerous Trade-off

But this passage reveals the dangerous psychological and social trade-off: when you pursue the elite, you implicitly communicate to everyone watching that elite status matters. Even if you personally don’t believe it, your actions teach it.

Slaves, poor people, and the marginalized who watched this exchange would have concluded: “The Prophet cares more about rich people than people like us.” Even though this wasn’t true in the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم heart, the optics would have taught this lesson.

Allah could not allow this impression to take root because Islam’s foundational message is universal human dignity regardless of social status.

Modern Applications: The Psychology of Celebrity and Influence

This same psychological pattern manifests today:

The Convert Celebrity Phenomenon

Muslim communities become disproportionately excited when someone famous accepts Islam or speaks well of Islam. The psychology is: “If this influential person validates us, we are validated.” This reveals:

  • Insecurity about our own faith needing external validation
  • Misplaced understanding of where value lies
  • Tendency to use people as trophies rather than recognize their genuine spiritual journey

Institutional Classism in Muslim Communities

The lecture identifies several modern manifestations:

In mosques during VIP visits: When mayors, professors, or media groups visit, volunteers are ordered around and treated as servants while VIPs receive special treatment. The irony is then performing prayer - the ultimate act of equality before Allah - specifically to demonstrate Islam to guests, while the treatment of actual Muslims contradicts the message.

In Islamic schools: Parents whose children memorize Quran sometimes develop superiority over parents whose children don’t, turning the Book meant to create humility into a source of arrogance and social hierarchy.

In fundraising: Those who donate more receive better treatment, better seating, more attention - creating a culture where money determines value even in religious spaces.

The Psychology of Self-Sufficiency (Istighnaa)

“Amma man istaghna” describes a psychological state: feeling no need for something beyond yourself. This manifests as:

Material Self-Sufficiency

“I have a job, health, family, happiness - what do I need religion for?” This reduces human existence to merely physical and emotional dimensions, denying the spiritual dimension entirely. It’s the psychology of someone who has never experienced a crisis that material means couldn’t solve, or who has suppressed awareness of existential questions.

Intellectual Self-Sufficiency

“I already know what I need to know.” This closes the door to growth, learning, and change. It’s the opposite of the learner’s mindset that characterized Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه.

Social Self-Sufficiency

“I don’t depend on anyone; everyone depends on me.” This is the mindset of those who set schedules rather than following them, who make decisions affecting others without consultation, who see themselves as above social norms that govern ordinary people.

The Quran doesn’t argue with this psychology - it simply states its reality and consequence: “wa ma ’alayka alla yazzakka” - you’re not responsible that he won’t purify himself. In other words, this psychological state makes spiritual growth impossible, and trying to change it is futile.

The Three Dimensions of Human Need

The lecture presents a framework for understanding human beings as having three interconnected types of needs:

  1. Physical needs: Food, health, safety, shelter
  2. Emotional needs: Relationships, belonging, psychological well-being
  3. Spiritual needs: Purpose, connection to the Divine, moral framework

These are interconnected: missing lunch (physical) makes you cranky (emotional) which ruins your concentration in prayer (spiritual). But someone in a state of istighnaa believes that satisfying physical and emotional needs is sufficient, remaining blind to the spiritual dimension.

This creates a population of people who appear successful by material standards but are spiritually starving - and don’t even recognize their starvation.

Political Implications: Who Gets Access to Leadership

The passage has profound implications for how religious leadership should allocate time and attention:

Traditional Political Calculus

Reach the influential because they influence others. This is strategically sound and Islamically acknowledged (prophets did approach rulers). But:

Islamic Value Calculus

The one who fears Allah and seeks purification deserves priority, regardless of social status. This creates a counter-cultural leadership model where:

  • Access isn’t determined by wealth or status
  • The spiritually hungry receive attention even when socially “unimportant”
  • Leadership must be careful not to create even the appearance of favoritism based on worldly criteria

The Leadership Dilemma

Leaders today face the same tension the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم faced: - How to engage with influential people (politicians, media, academics) without appearing to value them more - How to maintain accessibility to ordinary people while managing limited time - How to avoid creating class hierarchies even unintentionally

The solution isn’t to ignore the influential, but to ensure that in one-on-one interactions and in public settings, every person feels equally valued based on their taqwa and sincerity, not their social credentials.

Quranic Wisdom

The Primacy of Spiritual Value Over Social Status

The opening of Surah Abasa establishes one of the Quran’s most counter-cultural principles: true value in Allah’s sight has absolutely no correlation with social status, wealth, political influence, or worldly power. A blind man with no social standing who fears Allah is infinitely more valuable than the wealthiest, most powerful person who considers himself self-sufficient from Allah.

This turns human hierarchies upside down. We instinctively assign value based on: - How much someone can do for us - How influential they are in society - How their association might benefit us - What status proximity to them confers

But Allah assigns value based solely on taqwa (consciousness and fear of Allah) and desire for spiritual growth. “Inna akramakum ’inda Allahi atqakum” - the most noble of you in Allah’s sight are the most righteous.

The Mercy in Divine Correction

Notice the profound mercy in how Allah corrected His beloved Messenger صلى الله عليه وسلم:

  1. Immediate correction: The revelation came right then and there, preventing any lasting harm from the incident

  2. Public correction: Though potentially embarrassing, this public correction ensured everyone present learned the lesson - not just the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم

  3. Gentle language: Despite being a rebuke, the language is measured. Allah doesn’t say the Prophet “scowled” or “acted inappropriately” - He says he showed the slightest frown (’abasa)

  4. Contextualized understanding: Allah explains the internal spiritual realities invisible to human perception, teaching rather than just condemning

  5. Multiple possibilities acknowledged: “Perhaps he seeks to purify himself, or perhaps he will take reminder” - Allah acknowledges uncertainty about outcomes while correcting the prioritization

This models how we should correct one another: with precision about the issue, gentleness in delivery, and focus on teaching the principle.

The Wisdom of Not Knowing Hearts

“Wa ma yudrika” - what would make you know? This question enshrines a liberating truth: we cannot know what’s in people’s hearts. Only Allah knows.

The wisdom here is multifold:

Protection from Arrogance

If we could see into hearts, we would judge people with certainty and potentially become arrogant about our assessments. Our inability to know for certain keeps us humble.

Obligation to Give Benefit of Doubt

Since we can’t know someone’s internal spiritual state from their external behavior, we’re obligated to give benefit of the doubt. The socially awkward person might be the most sincere. The one who seems disruptive might be the most eager to learn.

Hope for Everyone

We cannot write anyone off as hopeless because we don’t know what Allah knows. The person who seems furthest from guidance might be closer than we think, or might be one moment away from transformation.

Humility in Dawah

When doing dawah, we cannot know who will benefit. The person who argues most might be processing deeply. The person who seems disinterested might be the one whose heart changes. We plant seeds without knowing which will grow.

The Timeless Value of Knowledge-Seeking

“Laa’llahu yazzakka aw yadhdhakkaru fa tanfa’uhu al-dhikra” teaches us about how knowledge and reminder work:

Immediate vs. Delayed Benefit

Sometimes we benefit immediately from what we learn - it changes our behavior right away. But often, benefit comes later. We hear something at age 10 that we don’t apply until age 30 when circumstances make it relevant. Both are valid forms of benefit.

This should: - Encourage teachers: You don’t know when your words will bear fruit - Encourage students: Don’t dismiss what seems irrelevant now; store it for when it becomes relevant - Encourage parents: Seeds planted in children’s hearts germinate in Allah’s timing

The Process of Internal Change

Just because someone doesn’t change immediately doesn’t mean nothing is happening. Like a seed underground, transformation begins internally before manifesting externally. “Yadhdhakkaru” (he will take reminder) speaks to this process of something settling in the heart and mind, being pondered, considered, until eventually it shapes behavior.

The Power of Sincere Words

Even brief reminders carry weight. Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه didn’t ask for a lengthy lecture - just “teach me something from what Allah taught you.” Simple reminders, sincerely received, can transform lives. This is why the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم encouraged brief post-prayer reminders in mosques.

Universal Human Dignity and the Challenge of Disability

By choosing to emphasize “al-a’ma” (the blind one), Allah embeds several timeless lessons:

Recognition of Extra Effort

Those facing physical, mental, or social challenges exert more effort to accomplish what others do easily. This extra effort is seen and valued by Allah, even when invisible to people.

Dignity in Difference

Calling him “the blind one” isn’t degrading - it’s acknowledging reality while simultaneously honoring the extra determination required because of that reality. There’s no shame in disability; there’s honor in overcoming its challenges to pursue spiritual growth.

Obligation to Accommodate

If Allah values the extra effort of the impaired so highly, communities must make accommodation for them - not as burdensome obligation but as honor to serve those Allah honors. Wheelchair ramps, sign language interpretation, large-print materials - these aren’t costly inconveniences but opportunities to recognize divine priorities.

Beyond Visible Behavior

Just as the blind man’s visual impairment was visible, many people have invisible impairments (psychological, emotional, neurological) that affect their behavior in ways others can’t see. The principle teaches us to look beyond surface behavior to recognize underlying struggles and honor the effort being made.

The Danger of Felt Self-Sufficiency

“Amma man istaghna” warns against the most dangerous spiritual state: feeling no need for Allah. This manifests in various forms:

Material Comfort as Spiritual Barrier

When all physical and emotional needs are met, people often feel no need for spiritual fulfillment. “I’m happy, healthy, successful - what do I need religion for?” This reduces human existence to its material dimension, denying the spiritual hunger that defines our fitrah (natural disposition).

The Three-Dimensional Human

We are physical, emotional, AND spiritual beings. Satisfying only two dimensions while starving the third creates deep, often unconscious, existential emptiness. The spiritual dimension isn’t just an add-on - it’s what gives purpose and direction to the other two.

When physical needs override spiritual ones, we become animals. When emotional needs override spiritual ones, we become slaves to feelings. The spiritual dimension properly ordered puts the other two in their proper place.

Self-Sufficiency as Blindness

The person who feels self-sufficient is actually blind to their deepest need, while the physically blind man could see clearly what truly mattered. True basira (insight) and true blindness are matters of the heart, not the eyes.

The Proper Role of Religious Leadership

This passage defines what religious leadership should prioritize:

Serve the Seekers, Not the Influential

Leadership should allocate attention based on sincerity and spiritual hunger, not social status. The person with no credentials but genuine desire to learn deserves more attention than the VIP who doesn’t value what’s being offered.

Beware of Optics

Even if your heart is pure, your actions teach lessons. If people see you consistently prioritizing the wealthy and powerful, they learn that Islam values wealth and power - regardless of what you verbally teach. Actions speak louder than words.

No One is Dismissed

From the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم permanent change in behavior toward Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum رضي الله عنه - always asking if he needed anything, treating him as a VIP, even appointing him governor of Madinah - we learn that no one should ever be made to feel dismissed or unimportant.

The Wisdom in Not Forcing Hearts

“Wa ma ’alayka alla yazzakka” - you’re not responsible that he doesn’t purify himself. This liberates us from the burden of thinking we must convert everyone:

Plant Seeds, Don’t Force Fruit

Our job is to convey the message with excellence, wisdom, and beauty. We plant seeds. Whether those seeds grow, when they grow, and how they grow is in Allah’s hands. This removes the paralyzing pressure to “succeed” in dawah according to conversion metrics.

Some Hearts are Sealed

Allah alone knows when a heart is truly sealed versus when it’s simply not ready yet. We cannot know, so we cannot give up on anyone while they’re alive. But we also don’t exhaust ourselves endlessly on those who show no receptivity when there are others genuinely seeking.

Prioritize the Receptive

Time and energy are limited. While we don’t write anyone off, we prioritize those showing receptivity and sincerity. It’s better to deeply benefit ten sincere seekers than to superficially engage with a hundred who aren’t ready.

The Equality of Salah

The lecture powerfully notes the irony: hosting VIPs at the mosque, treating volunteers poorly, then demonstrating Islam through prayer - the very act that symbolizes absolute equality before Allah. In salah:

  • No assigned seats based on status
  • All facing the same direction
  • All performing the same actions
  • All with heads on the ground in equal humility
  • Rows determined by who arrives first, not who’s most important

This is the practical, five-times-daily reminder that before Allah, all human hierarchies dissolve. We are all just slaves. This shouldn’t just be ritual - it should shape how we treat each other outside salah.

The Lifetime Impact of One Correction

From one brief moment of slight frustration, Allah created: - Eternal Quranic guidance for all humanity - Permanent elevation of Abdullah Ibn Umm Maktum’s رضي الله عنه status
- A complete reorientation of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم approach to all impaired people - A model for how Muslim societies should value people - Protection of Islamic egalitarian principles from being corrupted by classism

This shows how Allah uses moments - even uncomfortable ones - to teach eternal lessons and protect His religion’s core values.

Ayah 11–42 · linked video: 02. ’Abasa (Ayah 11-42)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 11

كَلَّا (kalla) [ك-ل-ل]

The root ك-ل-ل conveys the meaning of rejection, negation, or stopping someone from a false notion. The word كَلَّا (kalla) is a particle of emphatic rejection meaning “No! Not at all!” It serves to completely dismiss what preceded it. In this context, it rejects the attitude of those who feel they don’t need revelation - Allah is saying “absolutely not, you cannot treat revelation this way.”

تَذْكِرَةٌ (tadhkirah) [ذ-ك-ر]

The root ذ-ك-ر means to remember, mention, or bring to mind. The form تَذْكِرَةٌ (tadhkirah) is a noun meaning “reminder” or “remembrance.” This pattern emphasizes the continuous nature of the reminder. It’s not introducing something new but recalling what is already known - reminding people of their origin, their purpose, and their accountability. The word implies that the Quran reminds us of our own history, the legacy of humanity, and provides a moral spiritual view of history.

Ayah 13

صُحُفٍ (suhuf) [ص-ح-ف]

The root ص-ح-ف relates to pages, sheets, or scrolls. The word صُحُفٍ (suhuf) means scrolls or scriptures - the written pages that contain revelation. The plural form indicates multiple scrolls or scriptures, emphasizing the comprehensive nature of revelation.

مُكَرَّمَةٍ (mukarramah) [ك-ر-م]

The root ك-ر-م means nobility, generosity, and honor. The form مُكَرَّمَةٍ (mukarramah) means dignified, honored, or noble. The pattern (mufaʿʿal) indicates an intensive quality - these are scrolls that have been honored and dignified. This contrasts with those who think they carry dignity through wealth or status - true dignity lies in these scrolls.

Ayah 14

مَرْفُوعَةٍ (marfuʿah) [ر-ف-ع]

The root ر-ف-ع means to raise, elevate, or lift up. The form مَرْفُوعَةٍ (marfuʿah) means elevated or raised high. This passive participle indicates that Allah has elevated these scriptures to a high status. For those who feel they have high status in the land, the revelation is higher and puts them in their place.

مُطَهَّرَةٍ (mutahharah) [ط-ه-ر]

The root ط-ه-ر means to purify, cleanse, or make pure. The form مُطَهَّرَةٍ (mutahharah) means purified or cleansed. The intensive pattern indicates thorough purification. The scripture itself is purified and comes to purify those who feel they are clean - it puts them in their place by showing them true purity.

Ayah 15

سَفَرَةٍ (safarah) [ס-ف-ר]

The root س-ف-ر has multiple interconnected meanings: to unveil, expose, clarify, travel, or serve as an ambassador. The word سَفَرَةٍ (safarah) refers to scribes, writers, or ambassadors. In this context, it refers to angels who are the writers of revelation - they transcribe from the original manuscript with Allah and prepare the exact copy to be delivered. They are also ambassadors from Allah to the messengers. The core meaning of unveiling connects all uses - writing unveils knowledge, an ambassador unveils a message from a king, and travel unveils new territories. These angels unveil and expose the treasure of revelation to humanity.

كِرَامٍ (kiram) [ك-ر-م]

From the same root ك-ر-م as مُكَرَّمَةٍ, the word كِرَامٍ (kiram) means noble or dignified ones. The plural form emphasizes that all these angels possess nobility and honor.

بَرَرَةٍ (bararah) [ب-ر-ر]

The root ب-ر-ر means righteousness, goodness, and piety. The word بَرَرَةٍ (bararah) means righteous ones. This form is specifically used for angels, while أَبْرَار (abrar) is used for righteous people. The linguistic difference is that بَرَرَةٍ indicates many good ones - angels have far more good than people do, and all angels are essentially good.

Ayah 17

قُتِلَ (qutila) [ق-ت-ل]

The root ق-ت-ل means to kill or slay. The form قُتِلَ (qutila) is a passive verb meaning “may he be killed” or “cursed be he.” This is an expression of severe condemnation and frustration, emphasizing how extreme the human being’s ingratitude and arrogance is.

الْإِنْسَانُ (al-insan) [أ-ن-س]

The root أ-ن-س relates to human beings and companionship. The word الْإِنْسَانُ (al-insan) means “the human being” or “mankind.” The definite article makes it generic, referring to the human being in general who exhibits ingratitude.

أَكْفَرَهُ (akfarahu) [ك-ف-ر]

The root ك-ف-ر means to disbelieve, reject, or be ungrateful. The form أَكْفَرَهُ (akfarahu) is an exclamation meaning “how ungrateful he is!” or “how much he disbelieves!” The pattern (af’ala) expresses amazement at the intensity of the quality.

Ayah 18

شَيْءٍ (shay’) [ش-ي-ء]

The root ش-ي-ء means a thing or something. The word شَيْءٍ (shay’) means “what thing.” This is used sarcastically - Allah knows what humans are created from, but He asks rhetorically to emphasize the humble origin.

خَلَقَهُ (khalaqahu) [خ-ل-ق]

The root خ-ل-ق means to create, fashion, or bring into being. The verb خَلَقَهُ (khalaqahu) means “He created him.” This emphasizes Allah’s active role in bringing humans into existence from nothing.

Ayah 19

نُطْفَةٍ (nutfah) [ن-ط-ف]

The root ن-ط-ف relates to a drop or small amount of liquid. The word نُطْفَةٍ (nutfah) means a drop of sperm or seminal fluid. This is deliberately graphic and uncomfortable - Allah describes the sexual act and its product to humble those who think they are above needing revelation. It reminds them of their humiliating origin.

قَدَّرَهُ (qaddarahu) [ق-د-ر]

The root ق-د-ر means to measure, determine, or decree precisely. The form قَدَّرَهُ (qaddarahu) with the doubled middle letter (Form II) indicates intensive precise calculation. Allah precisely calculated how many bones, which limbs function, eye color, height, bone structure - every physical detail as the baby develops inside the mother’s womb. This is different from خَرْص (wild guessing) - it’s exact determination.

Ayah 20

السَّبِيلَ (as-sabil) [س-ب-ل]

The root س-ب-ل means a way, path, or road. The word السَّبِيلَ (as-sabil) means “the way” or “the road.” In this context, it refers to the birth canal - the passageway from the mother’s womb to the outside world.

يَسَّرَهُ (yassarahu) [ي-س-ر]

The root ي-س-ر means to make easy, facilitate, or smooth. The verb يَسَّرَهُ (yassarahu) means “He facilitated it” or “He made it easy.” Allah opened and facilitated the roadway for the child to come out. Without Allah opening that door, the child would die inside the mother.

Ayah 21

أَمَاتَهُ (amatahu) [م-و-ت]

The root م-و-ت means to die or cause to die. The verb أَمَاتَهُ (amatahu) in Form IV means “He caused him to die” or “He gave him death.” This emphasizes that death is actively given by Allah.

أَقْبَرَهُ (aqbarahu) [ق-ب-ر]

The root ق-ب-ر relates to graves and burial. The verb أَقْبَرَهُ (aqbarahu) in Form IV means “He caused him to be buried” or “He placed him in the grave.” This shows the human going from one belly (the mother’s womb) into another belly (the earth). The earth was called a cradle earlier in the Quran - it hugs you like a mother.

Ayah 22

شَاءَ (sha’a) [ش-ي-ء]

The root ش-ي-ء relates to will, desire, or intention. The verb شَاءَ (sha’a) means “He willed” or “He decided.” This emphasizes Allah’s complete authority over resurrection.

أَنْشَرَهُ (ansharahu) [ن-ش-ر]

The root ن-ش-ر means to spread out, unfold, or sprout. The verb أَنْشَرَهُ (ansharahu) means “He raised him” or “He spread him out again.” This suggests sprouting up again like a plant - resurrection from the earth.

Ayah 23

يَقْضِ (yaqdi) [ق-ض-ي]

The root ق-ض-ي means to finish, complete, execute, or fulfill. The verb يَقْضِ (yaqdi) in the jussive mood means “he has not fulfilled” or “he has not completed.” The word قَضَى means to be completely finished with a task. The human hasn’t yet executed what he was commanded - hasn’t finished his assignment of responding to revelation and becoming humble before Allah.

أَمَرَهُ (amarahu) [أ-م-ر]

The root أ-م-ر means to command or order. The verb أَمَرَهُ (amarahu) means “He commanded him” - what Allah commanded him to do, which is to respond to revelation.

Ayah 24

يَنْظُرِ (yanzur) [ن-ظ-ر]

The root ن-ظ-ر means to look, observe, or contemplate. The verb يَنْظُرِ (yanzur) means “let him look” or “he should look.” This implies taking a close, careful look with reflection.

طَعَامِهِ (ta’amihi) [ط-ع-م]

The root ط-ع-م relates to food, taste, and eating. The word طَعَامِهِ (ta’amihi) means “his food” or “his meal.” Allah directs humans to examine their food to understand their humble dependence.

Ayah 25

صَبَبْنَا (sababna) [ص-ب-ب]

The root ص-ب-ب means to pour forth or pour down. The verb صَبَبْنَا (sababna) means “We poured.” This indicates pouring down rain heavily and intensely.

الْمَاءَ (al-ma’) [م-ي-ه/م-و-ه]

The root relates to water and liquids. The word الْمَاءَ (al-ma’) means “the water” - specifically referring to rain.

صَبًّا (sabban) [ص-ب-ب]

From the same root ص-ب-ب, this is an intensive verbal noun meaning “a pouring” - emphasizing the intensity and abundance of the rain.

Ayah 26

شَقَقْنَا (shaqaqna) [ش-ق-ق]

The root ش-ق-ق means to split, crack open, or cleave. The verb شَقَقْنَا (shaqaqna) means “We split” or “We opened.” Allah caused the earth to crack open by means of the rain.

الْأَرْضَ (al-ard) [أ-ر-ض]

The root أ-ر-ض means earth, land, or ground. The word الْأَرْضَ (al-ard) means “the earth.” This parallels the opening of the womb for birth.

شَقًّا (shaqqan) [ش-ق-ق]

From the same root, this verbal noun means “a splitting” or “a cleaving” - emphasizing the action of opening.

Ayah 27

أَنْبَتْنَا (anbatna) [ن-ب-ت]

The root ن-ب-ت means to sprout, grow, or cause to grow. The verb أَنْبَتْنَا (anbatna) in Form IV means “We caused to sprout” or “We made grow.” Allah caused the seed inside the earth to sprout.

حَبًّا (habban) [ح-ب-ب]

The root ح-ب-ب relates to seeds, grains, or love. The word حَبًّا (habban) means “seed” or “grain.” This is the fundamental origin of all plant growth mentioned afterward.

Ayah 28

عِنَبًا (inaban) [ع-ن-ب]

The root ع-ن-ب relates to grapes. The word عِنَبًا (inaban) means “grapes.” This represents sweet, exotic, expensive fruit that can be misused (for wine). It symbolizes luxury versus necessity.

قَضْبًا (qadban) [ق-ض-ب]

The root ق-ض-ب means to cut or what can be cut. The word قَضْبًا (qadban) means “vegetables” or “herbs” - vegetation that animals eat the top of and humans pull out and clean. This represents necessary, common, inexpensive sustenance.

Ayah 29

زَيْتُونًا (zaytunan) [ز-ي-ت]

The root ز-ي-ت relates to olives and olive oil. The word زَيْتُونًا (zaytunan) means “olives.” Olive oil is the most important oil for Arabs - used for food enhancement, medicine, and digestion. It represents enhancement of life.

نَخْلًا (nakhlan) [ن-خ-ل]

The root ن-خ-ل relates to palm trees and dates. The word نَخْلًا (nakhlan) means “palm trees” - not just dates but the entire tree. Every part is used: the date, seed, bark, leaves - for shelter, clothes, weapons, flour, oil. This represents complete utility and necessity.

Ayah 30

حَدَائِقَ (hada’iqa) [ح-د-ق]

The root ح-د-ق means to surround or encircle. The word حَدَائِقَ (hada’iqa) means “gardens” - enclosed, cultivated gardens that surround you.

غُلْبًا (ghulban) [غ-ل-ب]

The root غ-ل-ب means to overcome or overwhelm. The word غُلْبًا (ghulban) means “dense” or “overwhelming” - gardens so thick with greenery that they overwhelm you. You’re inside a different world of greenery.

Ayah 31

فَاكِهَةً (fakihatan) [ف-ك-ه]

The root ف-ك-ه relates to fruits and joy. The word فَاكِهَةً (fakihatan) means “fruits.” The root also connects to joy and happiness.

أَبًّا (abban) [أ-ب-ب]

The root أ-ب-ب relates to pasture or grass. The word أَبًّا (abban) means “grass” or “pasture.” This is vegetation that provides soft ground in gardens and food for grazing animals.

Ayah 32

مَتَاعًا (mata’an) [م-ت-ع]

The root م-ت-ع means to enjoy, provide benefit, or give temporary pleasure. The word مَتَاعًا (mata’an) means “provision” or “enjoyment” - something that serves a purpose and provides utility.

أَنْعَامِكُمْ (an’amikum) [ن-ع-م]

The root ن-ع-م relates to livestock, cattle, and blessings. The word أَنْعَامِكُمْ (an’amikum) means “your cattle” or “your livestock.” These are calm, slow animals with minimal reflexes - the most docile of creatures.

Ayah 33

الصَّاخَّةُ (as-sakhkhah) [ص-خ-خ]

The root ص-خ-خ means to make a loud, deafening noise. The word الصَّاخَّةُ (as-sakhkhah) means “the deafening blast” or “the loud scream.” This refers to the sound of the trumpet on Judgment Day that deafens people because of its intensity - like the ringing in your ears after an explosion.

Ayah 34

يَفِرُّ (yafirru) [ف-ر-ر]

The root ف-ر-ر means to flee, run away in terror, or escape. The verb يَفِرُّ (yafirru) means “he will flee” or “he will run away.” This describes running with terror - like when an animal chases you or when running from danger.

الْمَرْءُ (al-mar’u) [م-ر-أ]

The root م-ر-أ relates to manhood, chivalry, and taking care of others. The word الْمَرْءُ (al-mar’u) doesn’t just mean “a person” - it means someone with dignity who cares for others, someone with chivalry. The one who helps the old lady across the street has مُرُوءَة (muruwwah).

أَخِيهِ (akhihi) [أ-خ-و]

The root أ-خ-و means brother, sibling, or close companion. The word أَخِيهِ (akhihi) means “his brother.” In Arabic, this doesn’t just mean sibling but also friend, companion, or partner - anyone close to you.

Ayah 35

أُمِّهِ (ummihi) [أ-م-م]

The root أ-م-م relates to mother and origin. The word أُمِّهِ (ummihi) means “his mother” - the one who gave birth to him.

أَبِيهِ (abihi) [أ-ب-و]

The root أ-ب-و means father. The word أَبِيهِ (abihi) means “his father.” Together, mother and father represent the strongest bond from birth.

Ayah 36

صَاحِبَتِهِ (sahibatihi) [ص-ح-ب]

The root ص-ح-ب means to accompany or be a companion. The word صَاحِبَتِهِ (sahibatihi) means “his companion” - specifically his wife. Allah didn’t say امْرَأَتِهِ (his woman) but صَاحِبَتِهِ, emphasizing the companionship and love - the woman he wanted to be with all the time, missed when apart, kept texting.

بَنِيهِ (banihi) [ب-ن-ي]

The root ب-ن-ي means to build or sons/children. The word بَنِيهِ (banihi) means “his children” or “his sons.” Together with the wife, this represents the family bond developed later in life.

Ayah 37

امْرِئٍ (imri’in) [م-ر-أ]

From the same root as الْمَرْءُ earlier, meaning a person or individual. This emphasizes that each person is alone on Judgment Day.

يَوْمَئِذٍ (yawma’idhin) [ي-و-م]

The root ي-و-م means day. The compound يَوْمَئِذٍ (yawma’idhin) means “on that day” - specifically Judgment Day.

شَأْنٌ (sha’nun) [ش-أ-ن]

The root ش-أ-ن means concern, affair, or matter. The word شَأْنٌ (sha’nun) means “a preoccupation” or “a concern” - something that only you can worry about, that concerns only you and nobody else. The same word is used for Allah in وَيَسْأَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ - there are things only Allah can do.

يُغْنِيهِ (yughnihi) [غ-ن-ي]

The root غ-ن-ي means to be free from need, independent, or wealthy. The verb يُغْنِيهِ (yughnihi) means “it makes him independent” or “it frees him from need.” Each person’s preoccupation will make them completely independent of caring about anyone else - parents, spouse, children, friends won’t matter.

Ayah 38

وُجُوهٌ (wujuhun) [و-ج-ه]

The root و-ج-ه means face, countenance, or direction. The word وُجُوهٌ (wujuhun) means “faces.” The face is where emotions and inner states are most visible.

يَوْمَئِذٍ (yawma’idhin) [ي-و-م]

Again, “on that day” - Judgment Day.

مُسْفِرَةٌ (musfirah) [س-ف-ر]

The root س-ف-ر means to unveil, expose, or be bright. The word مُسْفِرَةٌ (musfirah) means “bright” or “shining” - like when morning becomes extra bright. These faces will be lit up, radiating light.

Ayah 39

ضَاحِكَةٌ (dahikah) [ض-ح-ك]

The root ض-ح-ك means to laugh or smile. The word ضَاحِكَةٌ (dahikah) means “laughing” - unable to contain their joy. This describes the state of those seeing Allah.

مُسْتَبْشِرَةٌ (mustabshirah) [ب-ش-ر]

The root ب-ش-ر relates to skin, good news, and human beings. The word مُسْتَبْشِرَةٌ (mustabshirah) means “rejoicing” or “feeling congratulated.” When the skin is illuminated with joy, when someone looks radiant - this is استبشار. It can only make sense when someone important congratulates you - Allah is congratulating them, welcoming them.

Ayah 40

وُجُوهٌ (wujuhun) [و-ج-ه]

Again, “faces” - contrasting with the bright faces.

عَلَيْهَا (alayha) [ع-ل-و]

The root ع-ل-و means above or upon. The phrase عَلَيْهَا (alayha) means “upon them/it.”

غَبَرَةٌ (ghabarah) [غ-ب-ر]

The root غ-ب-ر relates to dust, remainder, or being left behind. The word غَبَرَةٌ (ghabarah) means “dust” - faces covered in dust. When the face is covered in dust, the eyes cannot see properly.

Ayah 41

تَرْهَقُهَا (tarhaquha) [ر-ه-ق]

The root ر-ه-ق means to overcome, overtake, or cover. The verb تَرْهَقُهَا (tarhaquha) means “it will cover them” or “it will overcome them.”

قَتَرَةٌ (qatarah) [ق-ت-ر]

The root ق-ت-ر has multiple meanings: black smoke/smut, stupidity, being accused/alleged, and being inclined toward evil. It also relates to bankruptcy (someone who looks darkened by their financial ruin). The word قَتَرَةٌ (qatarah) means “black smoke” or “darkness.” This is black smoke that covers their faces - you can tell these people are guilty by the look on their face. Depression and guilt literally change complexion. Some argue this is the smoke from hellfire already covering them; others say Allah doesn’t want to see them and doesn’t want them to see Him, so they’re surrounded by dark smoke.

Ayah 42

الْكَفَرَةُ (al-kafarah) [ك-ف-ر]

The root ك-ف-ر means to disbelieve, cover, reject, or be ungrateful. The word الْكَفَرَةُ (al-kafarah) means “the disbelievers” - those who rejected faith.

الْفَجَرَةُ (al-fajarah) [ف-ج-ر]

The root ف-ج-ر means to explode, act wickedly, or do as one pleases. The word الْفَجَرَةُ (al-fajarah) means “the wicked ones” or “those who do whatever they want.” A فَاجِر is someone who explodes into whatever behavior or words without concern for consequences - they do whatever they want whenever they want. The morning is called فَجْر because the light explodes.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayahs 11-12

The passage begins with the emphatic particle كَلَّا (kalla), which completely rejects and negates what preceded. This serves as a powerful transition from the opening story of the blind man and the wealthy man. The grammatical structure shifts from narrative past tense to present declarative statements, elevating the discourse.

The demonstrative pronoun إِنَّهَا (innaha - “indeed it”) in “إِنَّهَا تَذْكِرَةٌ” creates emphasis through the particle إِنَّ, stressing that the Quran is definitively a reminder. The feminine pronoun refers back to the revelation being discussed.

The conditional structure “فَمَن شَاءَ ذَكَرَهُ” (faman sha’a dhakarahu - “whoever wants may remember it”) uses the perfect tense شَاءَ to indicate a completed decision or willingness, followed by the perfect tense ذَكَرَهُ, creating a cause-and-effect relationship. This structure emphasizes human free will and choice regarding revelation.

Ayahs 13-16

The phrase “فِي صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ” uses the preposition فِي (in/within) to indicate the location of the revelation. The indefinite form صُحُفٍ creates a sense of magnitude and plurality.

The string of feminine plural adjectives مُّكَرَّمَةٍ (dignified), مَّرْفُوعَةٍ (elevated), مُّطَهَّرَةٍ (purified) creates a rhythmic, ascending emphasis. Each adjective is in the passive participle form, indicating that Allah is the one who has honored, elevated, and purified these scriptures.

The phrase “بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ” (bi-aydi safaratin) uses the preposition بِ to indicate agency or means - “by the hands of” or “in the possession of.” The dual form أَيْدِي (hands) adds emphasis to the physical, trusted nature of the angels’ role.

The adjectives كِرَامٍ بَرَرَةٍ are placed after سَفَرَةٍ without a conjunction, creating asyndetic coordination that adds weight to each quality. Both are in the plural form, matching سَفَرَةٍ.

Ayah 17

The passive verb قُتِلَ (may he be killed) is a rhetorical device expressing severe condemnation. This is an Arabic idiom used to express amazement at someone’s extreme behavior.

The interrogative structure “مَا أَكْفَرَهُ” (ma akfarahu - “how ungrateful he is!”) uses the exclamatory particle مَا with the Form IV verb أَكْفَرَ to create an exclamation of astonishment. The هُ suffix refers back to الْإِنسَانُ.

Ayahs 18-22

The rhetorical question “مِنْ أَيِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ” (from what thing did He create him?) uses the interrogative أَيِّ for dramatic effect. Allah knows the answer, but the question forces reflection.

The answer “مِن نُّطْفَةٍ” creates a stark, humbling image through its explicitness. The preposition مِنْ indicates source or origin.

The series of verbs خَلَقَهُ (He created him), قَدَّرَهُ (He determined him), يَسَّرَهُ (He facilitated for him), أَمَاتَهُ (He gave him death), أَقْبَرَهُ (He buried him), أَنشَرَهُ (He will resurrect him) creates a powerful rhetorical progression. Each verb is in the perfect tense except أَنشَرَهُ, which uses the future, marking resurrection as yet to come. The repeated هُ pronoun suffix creates anaphora, hammering home Allah’s complete control over every stage.

The phrase “ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ” (then the path He facilitated for him) uses fronting of the object السَّبِيلَ before the verb يَسَّرَهُ for emphasis - the “path” is highlighted before the action.

The particle ثُمَّ (then) between stages adds a sense of sequence and inevitability. The phrase “إِذَا شَاءَ أَنشَرَهُ” (whenever He wills, He will resurrect him) uses إِذَا to indicate a definite future event.

Ayah 23

The emphatic negation “كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ” uses كَلَّا again to reject and لَمَّا (not yet) with the jussive mood يَقْضِ to indicate an action that should have been completed but hasn’t. This creates urgency.

The phrase “مَا أَمَرَهُ” uses the relative pronoun/particle مَا to introduce what was commanded. The perfect tense أَمَرَهُ indicates a command already given.

Ayahs 24-32

The command “فَلْيَنظُرِ الْإِنسَانُ” uses the jussive form لْيَنظُرِ with the prefixed فَ creating an imperative: “So let man look!” This shifts to direct address and command.

The construction “إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِ” uses the preposition إِلَىٰ (toward/at) to direct the gaze specifically at food.

The subsequent passage (ayahs 25-31) uses the royal “We” (أَنَّا, صَبَبْنَا, شَقَقْنَا, أَنبَتْنَا, etc.) throughout, emphasizing Allah’s majesty and power. Each verb is in the perfect tense, indicating completed actions.

The phrase “أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا الْمَاءَ صَبًّا” uses the cognate accusative صَبًّا (an intense pouring) with the verb صَبَبْنَا to emphasize the intensity of the rain. This figure of speech (al-maf’ul al-mutlaq) strengthens the verb’s meaning.

Similarly, “شَقَقْنَا الْأَرْضَ شَقًّا” uses the cognate accusative for emphasis.

The list of vegetation (حَبًّا، عِنَبًا، قَضْبًا، زَيْتُونًا، نَخْلًا، حَدَائِقَ، فَاكِهَةً، أَبًّا) uses asyndetic coordination (no conjunctions) until the final وَأَبًّا, creating a rapid accumulation effect that builds to a climax. The items are all in the accusative case as objects of أَنبَتْنَا.

The phrase “حَدَائِقَ غُلْبًا” places the adjective غُلْبًا after the noun for emphasis - “gardens overwhelming.”

The final phrase “مَّتَاعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَامِكُمْ” uses the accusative of purpose/result (al-maf’ul li-ajlihi) - “as provision for you and your livestock.” The conjunction وَ coordinates لَّكُمْ and لِأَنْعَامِكُمْ, placing humans and animals side by side, which serves to humble humans.

Ayahs 33-37

The temporal phrase “فَإِذَا جَاءَتِ الصَّاخَّةُ” uses فَإِذَا (so when) to introduce the scene of Judgment Day. The definite article in الصَّاخَّةُ indicates this is a known, specific event.

The structure “يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ الْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ” (the day when a man will flee from his brother) uses the construct يَوْمَ as a badal (appositive) explaining when الصَّاخَّةُ comes. The preposition مِنْ indicates the source of fleeing - what one is running away from.

The series وَأُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ، وَصَاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ creates coordination through repeated وَ. The pairing structure (mother-father, wife-children) is rhetorically significant, organizing relationships into circles.

The phrase “لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ” uses لِكُلِّ (for every) to indicate universal application. The phrase يَوْمَئِذٍ is an adverbial time expression. The sentence structure places شَأْنٌ as the subject with يُغْنِيهِ as a descriptive relative clause modifying شَأْنٌ.

Ayahs 38-42

The phrase “وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّسْفِرَةٌ” uses a nominal sentence with indefinite وُجُوهٌ as the subject, creating a sense of some faces (not all). The adjective مُّسْفِرَةٌ follows the noun.

The two adjectives ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ are coordinated asyndetically (without وَ), creating a rapid, joyful succession.

The contrasting passage “وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ” uses وَ to coordinate with the previous scene, but introduces opposite imagery. The phrase عَلَيْهَا (upon them/it) indicates the location of the غَبَرَةٌ (dust).

The verb “تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ” has قَتَرَةٌ as the subject (that which covers) and تَرْهَقُهَا as the verb with pronominal object هَا referring back to the faces.

The demonstrative phrase “أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ” uses أُولَٰئِكَ (those) pointing to the faces just mentioned. The pronoun هُمُ creates emphasis before the predicate. The two descriptors الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ are coordinated asyndetically, both in the definite form with the definite article, identifying these specific people conclusively.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayahs 11-16

The description of revelation’s origin connects to the Islamic understanding of previous scriptures. The mention of صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ (honored scrolls) elevated and purified parallels the Quranic concept of an original heavenly book - the اللَّوْحِ الْمَحْفُوظِ (Preserved Tablet) mentioned elsewhere in the Quran. This is the source from which all divine revelations flow, including the Torah given to Musa (AS), the Psalms given to Dawud (AS), the Gospel given to Isa (AS), and the scrolls given to Ibrahim (AS).

The angels described as سَفَرَةٍ (scribes/ambassadors) who transcribe revelation connect to the role of Jibreel (AS), who is specifically charged with delivering revelation to the prophets. The Quran describes Jibreel elsewhere as رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (a noble messenger) and مُطَاعٍ (obeyed in the heavens), connecting to the description here of angels who are كِرَامٍ بَرَرَةٍ (noble and righteous).

The emphasis on revelation being مُّطَهَّرَةٍ (purified) addresses accusations made against previous scriptures. The Jews and Christians are referenced in hadith as having Torah and Gospel in their hands but not benefiting from them - they recite but don’t understand or implement. This passage establishes that the origin of revelation is pure; any corruption comes from human handling, not from the divine source.

Ayahs 17-23

The description of human creation from نُّطْفَةٍ (a drop of sperm) echoes earlier Quranic passages and parallels the Biblical account of human creation from humble origins. The Quran consistently reminds humans of their lowly beginnings to counter arrogance - this theme appears in Surah Al-Qiyamah, Surah Al-Mu’minun, and other surahs.

The precise determination (قَدَّرَهُ) of human development in the womb reflects both Quranic emphasis on Allah’s precise knowledge and creative power, and parallels passages in Psalms about God’s knowledge of human formation (“You knit me together in my mother’s womb” - Psalm 139).

The mention of السَّبِيلَ (the path/way) being facilitated has dual meanings: the physical birth canal and the spiritual path to guidance. This connects to the Quranic theme of Allah showing humanity the way - “We showed him the path, whether he be grateful or ungrateful” (76:3).

Ayahs 24-32

The command to look at food and its origins connects to Biblical themes of provision and gratitude. The description of rain, earth splitting, and vegetation growing parallels Genesis creation accounts and Psalms celebrating God’s provision (Psalm 104: “He makes grass grow for the cattle, and plants for people to cultivate”).

The specific mention of عِنَبًا (grapes) may allude to their use in previous scriptures - wine being permitted in Christianity but forbidden in Islam. The Quran acknowledges grapes as a blessing while prohibiting their fermentation.

The reference to زَيْتُونًا (olives) connects to the blessed olive tree mentioned elsewhere in the Quran (Surah An-Nur) and the importance of olive oil in Biblical lands - used for anointing, healing, and light.

The نَخْلًا (palm trees) are specifically Arabian and symbolize the Arab context of revelation, while also appearing in Biblical narratives of the Promised Land.

The ultimate message about food being مَّتَاعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَامِكُمْ (provision for you and your cattle) echoes Genesis themes of creation serving humanity, while humbling humans by placing them alongside animals in their need for sustenance.

Ayahs 33-37

The الصَّاخَّةُ (deafening blast) refers to the trumpet blown by Israfeel (AS) on Judgment Day, a concept present in Islamic eschatology and paralleling the trumpet/shofar in Biblical apocalyptic literature (1 Thessalonians 4:16, Revelation, Jewish eschatology).

The scene of people fleeing from loved ones echoes prophetic descriptions across traditions about the Day of Judgment being so terrible that normal human bonds dissolve. This parallels Jesus’s statement about families being divided (Matthew 10:35-36) and Old Testament prophecies about the Day of the Lord.

The specific mention of fleeing from أَخِيهِ (brother), أُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ (mother and father), صَاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ (wife and children) covers all primary human relationships, emphasizing the complete breakdown of worldly attachments.

Ayahs 38-42

The contrast between وُجُوهٌ مُّسْفِرَةٌ (bright faces) and وُجُوهٌ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ (faces covered in dust) parallels Biblical imagery of light versus darkness, righteousness versus wickedness. The righteous are described as shining like stars in Daniel 12:3, and Jesus speaks of the righteous shining like the sun in His Father’s kingdom (Matthew 13:43).

The dust and قَتَرَةٌ (black smoke/darkness) covering faces parallels Biblical imagery of shame, mourning, and separation from God’s presence - in Genesis, Adam and Eve hide from God after sin; in Revelation, those who worship the beast are tormented with fire and smoke.

The final identification of these faces as الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ (the disbelievers who did as they pleased) connects to Biblical warnings about the lawless and those who reject God’s commands across both Old and New Testaments.

Coherence

Connection to Surah Opening (Ayahs 1-10)

Ayahs 11-16 serve as a direct response to the opening narrative. The blind man came seeking knowledge of revelation, while the wealthy man dismissed it. These ayahs establish that revelation is not something to be taken lightly or dismissed - it is كَلَّا (absolutely not acceptable to treat it that way). The revelation originates from صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ مُّطَهَّرَةٍ (honored, elevated, purified scrolls), handled by noble righteous angels.

The connection is purposeful: if someone feels they are elite (مُسْتَغْنِي) and above needing guidance, these ayahs remind them that the revelation itself is elevated far above them. The one who thought he had كَرَامَة (dignity) through wealth is told that true كَرَامَة lies in the مُّكَرَّمَةٍ scriptures and the كِرَامٍ angels. The attitude shift required is from arrogance to humility before revelation’s grandeur.

Connection Between Revelation’s Status and Human Origin (Ayahs 17-23)

After establishing revelation’s high status, Allah immediately contrasts this with human origins. The transition through قُتِلَ الْإِنسَانُ مَا أَكْفَرَهُ (cursed be man, how ungrateful he is!) connects human arrogance to ingratitude. The question مِنْ أَيِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ (from what thing was he created?) forces reflection on the contrast between revelation’s nobility and humanity’s humble beginnings.

The journey from نُّطْفَةٍ (sperm drop) to قَبْر (grave) to نَشْر (resurrection) parallels the revelation’s journey from elevated scrolls through angelic scribes to human recipients. Both are journeys from higher to lower planes, but while revelation descends to elevate humanity, humans who reject it descend into disgrace.

The statement كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ مَا أَمَرَهُ (No! He has not yet fulfilled what He commanded him) directly addresses the wealthy man’s attitude in the opening - he hasn’t fulfilled his obligation to revelation, which is to receive it with humility and eagerness like the blind man did.

Parallel Journeys: Human Life and Food (Ayahs 24-32)

The command فَلْيَنظُرِ الْإِنسَانُ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِ (let man look at his food) creates a parallel between human creation and food production. Both begin with a drop (نُّطْفَةٍ for humans, water for crops), both involve earth splitting open (birth canal / soil), both produce life, and both return to earth.

This parallel serves multiple purposes in the surah’s coherence: 1. It makes the abstract lesson of human humility concrete and visible 2. It adds gratitude to humility as required responses to revelation 3. It reminds humans daily (through eating) of their dependence on Allah 4. It places humans alongside their أَنْعَام (cattle) in their need for sustenance, further humbling those who thought themselves elite

The food journey from rain to harvest mirrors the revelation journey from heavenly scrolls to human hearts - both are gifts descending from above that humans did nothing to earn but should be grateful for.

Transition to Judgment Day (Ayahs 33-37)

The word أَنْعَامِكُمْ (your cattle) at the end of the food passage strategically connects to الصَّاخَّةُ (the deafening blast) that begins the Judgment Day scene. Cattle are calm, slow, unreflective animals - but even they react to loud sounds by running. This creates the comparison: humans who didn’t reflect on revelation will react like cattle on Judgment Day, running in terror.

The theme of self-sufficiency (اسْتَغْنَىٰ) from the opening is inverted on Judgment Day. The wealthy man felt he didn’t need anyone or anything. On Judgment Day, لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ (every person will have a concern that makes him independent) - but this independence is isolation and terror, not the self-sufficient pride he felt in this life.

The fleeing from أَخِيهِ (brother), أُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ (mother-father), صَاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ (wife-children) represents the three circles of relationships that preoccupy humans in this life. The opening showed the Prophet ﷺ preoccupied with convincing the elite. On Judgment Day, the elite will be preoccupied with their own terror, unable to help anyone.

Two Categories of Faces: Resolution of Opening Scene (Ayahs 38-42)

The bright faces (وُجُوهٌ مُّسْفِرَةٌ ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ) represent those who approached revelation like the blind man - eager, humble, seeking. The word مُّسْفِرَةٌ (bright/unveiled) connects to سَفَرَةٍ (the scribes/angels who unveil revelation) from ayah 15 - those who sought to unveil revelation’s meanings in this life will have their faces unveiled and illuminated in the next.

The dust-covered faces (وُجُوهٌ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ) represent those who approached revelation like the wealthy man - dismissive, arrogant, feeling no need. The غَبَرَةٌ (dust) and قَتَرَةٌ (black smoke/darkness) contrasts with the مُّطَهَّرَةٍ (purified) scrolls from ayah 14 - they rejected purification in this life, so they’re covered in impurity in the next.

The identification as الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ (disbelievers who did whatever they wanted) directly answers who the مُسْتَغْنِي (self-sufficient one) was from the opening - he was among those who did what they wanted (الْفَجَرَةُ) without regard for Allah’s commands.

Surah Structure: Six-Part Symmetry

The surah’s overall structure creates a symmetrical coherence:

Part 1 (Ayahs 1-10): Two attitudes toward revelation (eager vs. dismissive) Part 2 (Ayahs 11-16): Revelation’s elevated noble status Part 3 (Ayahs 17-23): Human’s humble origins and journey Part 4 (Ayahs 24-32): Food’s journey paralleling human journey Part 5 (Ayahs 33-37): Judgment Day - everyone fleeing, preoccupied Part 6 (Ayahs 38-42): Two categories of faces (bright vs. dark)

Parts 1 and 6 mirror each other: two attitudes toward revelation result in two types of faces on Judgment Day.

Parts 2 and 5 mirror each other: The elite one felt self-sufficient (مُسْتَغْنِي) in Part 1; in Part 5, everyone has a concern that makes them “independent” (يُغْنِيهِ) but in terror. Revelation was مُّكَرَّمَةٍ (honored) in Part 2; humans thought they had honor, but on Judgment Day they have only شَأْنٌ (preoccupation/concern).

Parts 3 and 4 are the center, both describing journeys that begin with drops and involve earth opening - human life and food production as parallel lessons in humility and gratitude.

Connection to Surah Al-Nazi’at (Preceding Surah)

Surah An-Nazi’at ends with questions about the Hour and its arrival (يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ - “They ask you about the Hour”). Surah Abasa continues this eschatological theme by describing Judgment Day scenes (الصَّاخَّةُ, fleeing from relatives, two types of faces). Both surahs emphasize that the Hour will come suddenly and terribly.

An-Nazi’at mentions that faces on that day will be نَاضِرَةٌ (radiant) looking at their Lord, while Abasa describes faces that are مُّسْفِرَةٌ ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ (bright, laughing, rejoicing). Both describe the blessed seeing Allah and being in His light.

Thematic Links Throughout the Quran

The theme of human creation from humble origins appears throughout the Quran (23:12-14, 75:37-39, 76:2, 96:2) and always serves to humble human arrogance.

The description of revelation’s elevated status and angelic scribes connects to 56:77-80 (“Indeed it is a noble Quran, in a protected Book, none touch it except the purified”) and 85:21-22 (“Nay, it is a Glorious Quran, in a Preserved Tablet”).

The contrast between grateful and ungrateful humans is a major Quranic theme (76:3, 100:6-8, 99:6-8), always tied to acceptance or rejection of revelation.

The Judgment Day scene of people fleeing from loved ones appears in 70:10-14 with similar imagery, emphasizing this as a consistent eschatological teaching.

The food and provision theme as a sign of Allah’s mercy appears throughout the Quran (16:10-15, 36:33-35, 50:9-11), always calling for gratitude and reflection on Allah’s power.

Psychological, Political & Social

Human Nature and Self-Perception (Ayahs 17-23)

These ayahs provide deep psychological insight into human arrogance and self-deception. The question مَا أَكْفَرَهُ (how ungrateful/arrogant he is!) diagnoses a fundamental human psychological flaw: we forget our origins. Psychologically, when people achieve status or wealth, they often engage in what psychologists call “self-serving bias” - attributing success to their own abilities while forgetting circumstances, help received, and humble beginnings.

The graphic reminder of creation from نُّطْفَةٍ (sperm drop) serves as a psychological reset button. By forcing humans to remember their most vulnerable, dependent state, Allah breaks through the cognitive distortions of pride. The fetus in the womb has zero agency, zero control, zero achievement - everything is done to and for it. This reality check is meant to shatter the illusion of self-sufficiency.

The rapid progression from birth to death (أَمَاتَهُ فَأَقْبَرَهُ) creates psychological urgency. We live in the small window between these two powerless states, yet we act as if we’re permanent and powerful. This is what psychologists might call “terror management” gone wrong - instead of facing our mortality with humility, we construct elaborate delusions of significance.

The statement كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ مَا أَمَرَهُ (he hasn’t yet fulfilled what was commanded) speaks to human procrastination and avoidance. Psychologically, we avoid what makes us uncomfortable. Revelation makes the arrogant uncomfortable because it demands submission, so they avoid it while pretending they don’t need it.

Social Hierarchy and Class Dynamics (Ayahs 11-16, 24-32)

The opening context of this section addresses social stratification. The wealthy man felt his social status exempted him from needing guidance, while the blind poor man was eager to learn. This reflects a universal social dynamic: those with worldly power often feel entitled and above others, including above divine guidance.

The description of revelation as مُّكَرَّمَةٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ (honored, elevated) directly challenges class-based notions of dignity. True honor doesn’t come from wealth, lineage, or social position - it comes from connection to divine revelation. This is socially revolutionary, inverting the value system of Makkan society (and all hierarchical societies).

The comparison of humans to أَنْعَام (cattle) in terms of food needs is a great social equalizer. The elite may eat fancier meals, but they need food just like animals do. This physiological equality undermines social pretensions - your body has the same basic needs as everyone else’s, including animals’.

The detailed description of food production (rain, earth, crops) emphasizes interdependence and collective dependence on Allah. No human - regardless of wealth or status - produces their own food from nothing. The CEO and the beggar both depend on rain, soil, seeds, and sunshine. This creates a social leveling: we’re all dependent beings.

Family Bonds and Their Limits (Ayahs 34-37)

The Judgment Day scene of fleeing from family provides deep insight into human relationships and their ultimate fragility. In normal life, family relationships are considered our strongest bonds - parents sacrifice for children, spouses partner together, siblings support each other. These relationships define much of human social life and give people their sense of identity and security.

The three circles mentioned - friends (أَخِيهِ can mean friends), parents (أُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ), and spouse-children (صَاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ) - represent different types of social bonds: chosen relationships, birth relationships, and created relationships. Together they encompass the entirety of human social existence.

The fact that people will flee from all these relationships reveals a psychological truth: these bonds, while important, are ultimately worldly and temporary. People often invest their entire sense of meaning and security in family relationships, but these ayahs show that such bonds cannot save us in the ultimate accountability.

Psychologically, the phrase لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ (each person will have their own preoccupying concern) speaks to radical individual responsibility. We live in a social world where we can deflect, blame others, hide behind relationships, or derive identity from associations. On Judgment Day, all that social scaffolding collapses - you face yourself alone.

This has implications for how we should view relationships in this life: they’re important and blessed, but they shouldn’t become ultimate. Your relationship with Allah must be primary because that’s the only relationship that survives death and Judgment.

The Psychology of Reminder vs. New Information (Ayahs 11-12)

The word تَذْكِرَةٌ (reminder) rather than “new teaching” reveals something important about human psychology and learning. The Quran positions itself not as introducing foreign concepts but as reminding humans of what they innately know or should know.

Psychologically, this aligns with the concept of fitrah - humans have an innate disposition toward recognizing truth. Revelation reminds us of our purpose, our Creator, our mortality - things we know deep down but forget or suppress.

The voluntary nature emphasized in فَمَن شَاءَ ذَكَرَهُ (whoever wishes may remember it) respects human agency while placing responsibility on the individual. Psychologically, change requires willingness - you cannot force transformation. The Quran invites, reminds, and offers, but the decision to engage must come from within.

This also addresses the psychology of resistance. When people feel something is being forced on them, they resist. The Quran’s approach of offering itself as a reminder that people can freely accept removes that psychological barrier. Those who reject it cannot claim they were coerced - the rejection is their own choice.

The Politics of Revelation and Authority (Ayahs 11-16)

The elaboration of revelation’s origin and the angels’ role serves a political purpose in the Makkan context. The Quraysh elite claimed authority based on lineage, wealth, and tribal leadership. By establishing that revelation comes from the highest heaven through the noblest angels, Allah removes religious authority from human social structures and places it with the divine.

This has radical political implications: no human nobility or wealth gives authority to interpret or reject revelation. The blind poor man has the same access to truth as the wealthy leader - in fact, more access if he has more humility and eagerness.

The description of angels as كِرَامٍ بَرَرَةٍ (noble and righteous) establishes a different criterion for nobility - righteousness and service to Allah, not wealth or birth. This challenges the entire basis of Makkan social and political hierarchy.

The emphasis that revelation is مُّطَهَّرَةٍ (purified) and handled by purified beings means that humans cannot corrupt it with their political agendas or social biases. The message comes pure from the source; any distortion is human, not divine.

Gratitude, Entitlement, and Social Responsibility (Ayahs 24-32)

The command to look at food and reflect on its journey addresses the psychology of entitlement. Modern humans (and ancient wealthy ones) often take provisions for granted. We see food as something we earned through our work and money, forgetting the entire chain of divine provision that makes it possible.

Psychologically, gratitude is linked to well-being and humility, while entitlement is linked to dissatisfaction and arrogance. By forcing reflection on food’s origins, Allah cultivates gratitude, which in turn cultivates humility.

The mention of أَنْعَامِكُمْ (your cattle) alongside human provision has social implications: we’re responsible for animals under our care. This creates an ethic of stewardship and compassion extending beyond humans.

The detailed list of various food types (grapes, vegetables, olives, dates, fruits, grass) shows Allah’s provision includes both necessities and luxuries, both what sustains and what beautifies life. Socially, this means we should appreciate not just survival but the aesthetic dimensions of life Allah has provided.

The pairing of types - sweet grapes with necessary vegetables, enhancement olives with utility palm trees - teaches balance. A good society provides both necessities for all and beauties to enjoy, both utility and enhancement. This is a social-political philosophy embedded in ayahs about food.

The Psychology of Judgment Day Terror (Ayahs 33-37)

The الصَّاخَّةُ (deafening blast) creating such terror that people flee from loved ones reveals psychological truths about fear and self-preservation. In extreme fear, even our deepest social bonds become secondary to self-preservation. This is the psychological reality of overwhelming terror.

The progression from friend to parents to spouse/children - from most voluntary to most biological bonds - shows that in extreme conditions, all social constructions fail. Even the parent-child bond, considered strongest, dissolves under the weight of individual accountability.

Psychologically, this serves as a stark warning: whatever you’re avoiding in this life by hiding in relationships or social roles, you’ll face alone eventually. This should motivate individual moral development rather than relying on social identity.

The phrase يُغْنِيهِ (makes him independent/free from need) is ironic - humans in this life claim independence from Allah, but on Judgment Day they’ll have an independence they don’t want: isolation in their own accountability.

The Sociology of Belief and Disbelief (Ayahs 38-42)

The final division into two types of faces represents the ultimate social sorting - not by wealth, tribe, or family, but by belief and behavior. This is sociology on the grandest scale: all of human society ultimately divides into those who accepted truth with humility and those who rejected it with arrogance.

The bright, laughing, rejoicing faces represent a social group defined by spiritual rather than worldly criteria. Their joy is communal - they share the light of Allah’s presence together. This is the ultimate positive community.

The dust-covered, smoke-darkened faces represent those identified as الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ (disbelievers who did whatever they wanted). Sociologically, they represent a community of lawlessness and rejection of moral authority. Their defining characteristic is فَجَرَة - doing whatever they wanted without regard for consequences or divine command.

This creates a social philosophy: true community and belonging come through shared commitment to truth and righteousness, not through shared ethnicity, wealth, or worldly status. The only social division that ultimately matters is belief versus disbelief, submission versus rebellion.

The contrast also speaks to visible versus hidden realities. In this world, the disbelievers may have appeared clean, elevated, and dignified, while believers may have appeared lowly. On Judgment Day, the reality is unveiled - faces reveal inner spiritual states. This teaches that we should look at people’s spiritual state rather than their worldly appearance.

Quranic Wisdom

The True Nature of Revelation

These ayahs teach us that the Quran is not just another book - it is connected directly to the highest heavens, originating from صُحُفٍ مُّكَرَّمَةٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ مُّطَهَّرَةٍ (honored, elevated, purified scrolls). This should fundamentally change our relationship with the Quran. When we hold a mushaf, we’re holding words that angels handled, that come from the preserved heavenly book, that carry the dignity and purity of their divine source.

The practical application is that we must approach the Quran with awe, reverence, and humility - not as entertainment, not as mere recitation, and not as decoration. Every word should be pondered, every command should be implemented, every prohibition should be avoided. The Quran’s purpose is تَذْكِرَةٌ - to remind us, to wake us up, to keep us conscious of our reality and purpose.

The phrase فَمَن شَاءَ ذَكَرَهُ (whoever wishes may remember it) teaches that benefiting from the Quran requires willingness and active engagement. The Quran doesn’t force itself on anyone, but it’s available to all who genuinely seek. This is both empowering and sobering - we have agency to benefit or lose out.

The Reality of Human Existence

The graphic description of human creation from نُّطْفَةٍ (sperm drop) through precise development (قَدَّرَهُ) to facilitated birth (يَسَّرَهُ) to death (أَمَاتَهُ) to burial (أَقْبَرَهُ) to resurrection (أَنشَرَهُ) contains profound wisdom about who we are.

We are completely dependent beings at every stage. We didn’t choose our conception, our genetics, our birth, our lifespan, or our resurrection. This should create in us deep humility and gratitude. When we achieve anything, we should recognize it came through countless provisions and facilitations from Allah that we didn’t earn.

The wisdom here is that human arrogance is absurd. How can a being who began as a drop of fluid and will end up in a grave act arrogantly? How can someone who controls nothing about their own existence feel self-sufficient? This realization should make us constantly aware of our need for Allah.

The statement كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ مَا أَمَرَهُ (No! He has not yet fulfilled what He was commanded) creates urgency. We have a mission - to respond to revelation with submission and implementation. Life’s brevity means we should focus on this essential task rather than getting distracted by worldly pursuits that ultimately don’t matter.

The Lessons in Our Daily Sustenance

The command فَلْيَنظُرِ الْإِنسَانُ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِ (let man look at his food) teaches that spiritual lessons are embedded in our daily physical realities. Every meal should remind us:

  1. We’re completely dependent - we didn’t make the rain fall, the earth split, the seed grow, the fruit ripen
  2. Allah provides with both necessity and beauty - food isn’t just nutritious but also delicious, colorful, pleasant
  3. We share provisions with animals, which should humble us and create compassion for creatures under our care
  4. Everything in creation serves multiple purposes - the palm tree provides dates, shade, building materials, clothing fibers

The practical wisdom is to never eat mindlessly. Each meal is an opportunity to remember Allah, to be grateful, to feel humbled by our dependence, and to recognize His generosity in providing more than mere survival.

The pairing of عِنَبًا وَقَضْبًا (grapes and vegetables) - luxury and necessity - teaches that we should appreciate both aspects of provision. Don’t become so focused on necessities that you can’t enjoy Allah’s gifts, and don’t become so focused on luxuries that you forget basic needs and the needs of others.

The mention of زَيْتُونًا (olives) and نَخْلًا (palm trees) - enhancement and utility - teaches that life should have both: things that enhance and beautify, and things that serve essential functions. This is wisdom for building a balanced life.

The Nature of Accountability

The Judgment Day scene beginning with الصَّاخَّةُ (the deafening blast) teaches the inevitability and intensity of accountability. No matter how comfortable or established we feel in this life, that day will come and will be overwhelming.

The fleeing from loved ones - أَخِيهِ (brother/friend), أُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ (mother and father), صَاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ (wife and children) - teaches that all worldly relationships and social structures will collapse. The wisdom is that we should invest in what lasts: our individual relationship with Allah and our righteous deeds.

This doesn’t mean neglecting family and friends, but rather understanding their proper place. Maintain these relationships with excellence, but recognize they cannot save you on Judgment Day. Your ultimate reliance must be on Allah, and your ultimate investment must be in righteous action.

The phrase لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ (each person will have a concern that makes them independent) teaches radical individual responsibility. You cannot blame your upbringing, your society, your friends, your spouse. You will stand alone, and you will answer alone. This should make us very careful about our choices and very focused on personal accountability.

The Two Paths and Their Outcomes

The contrasting descriptions of وُجُوهٌ مُّسْفِرَةٌ ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ (bright, laughing, rejoicing faces) versus وُجُوهٌ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ (faces covered in dust and overcome by black smoke) teaches that life’s choices have real, visible consequences.

The blessed faces are described with light, laughter, and joy - these are people who will see Allah and be in His presence. The wisdom is that whatever you sacrifice in this life to please Allah - wealth, status, comfort, desires - will be more than compensated with eternal joy in His presence.

The wretched faces are covered in dust and darkness - separated from Allah’s light, unable to see properly, marked by their rejection and disobedience. The wisdom is that pursuing worldly pleasures and status while rejecting divine guidance leads to ultimate humiliation and loss.

The identification of the wretched as الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ (disbelievers who did as they pleased) teaches that the defining characteristic of those who fail is doing whatever they want without regard for divine command. The wisdom is that true freedom is not doing whatever you want, but disciplining yourself to do what Allah wants. Paradoxically, submitting to Allah is what liberates you.

The Proper Attitude Toward Revelation

Connecting back to the surah’s opening, these ayahs teach that the proper attitude toward revelation is eager receptivity, not dismissive arrogance. The blind man’s attitude - despite his worldly disadvantages - was superior because he recognized his need and sought knowledge.

The wisdom is that spiritual insight is not correlated with worldly status. The poorest person can be the richest in faith, and the richest person can be the poorest in faith. What matters is the heart’s orientation: is it humble and seeking, or arrogant and dismissive?

We should examine ourselves honestly: Do we treat the Quran like the elite Makkan treated the Prophet ﷺ - as if we’re doing Allah a favor by listening? Or do we approach it like the blind man - eager, humble, grateful for any crumb of guidance we can get?

The practical application is to study the Quran with meaning, to let it transform us, to prioritize it above worldly learning, and to recognize that it is the ultimate source of honor and guidance. Everything else - degrees, status, wealth, relationships - is secondary to this.

The Urgency of Life

The rapid progression from drop to grave to resurrection, with barely any mention of the life in between, teaches the brevity and urgency of our time in this world. كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ (No! He hasn’t fulfilled) creates a sense of unfinished business.

The wisdom is to not waste time. Don’t wait until you’re older or more established to start taking revelation seriously. Don’t assume you have decades to eventually get around to spiritual development. The window is short, and Allah has given you a mission: respond to His revelation with your life.

This should create a sense of purpose and focus. Among all the distractions and pursuits available in life, clarify what actually matters: Am I fulfilling what Allah commanded? Am I drawing closer to Him? Am I becoming who He wants me to be?

The Interconnectedness of Creation

The detailed description of how rain, earth, seeds, and various plants work together to produce sustenance teaches that creation is an interconnected system. Nothing exists in isolation - everything serves purposes beyond itself.

The wisdom for us is that we’re part of this interconnected whole. We depend on countless elements working together - air, water, soil, sun, plants, animals. This should create humility about our place in creation and gratitude for the intricate provisions Allah has arranged.

It should also create a sense of responsibility. If everything in creation is serving purposes and working together, we should ask: What is my role? How do I contribute to the good of the whole? Am I fulfilling my purpose in the divine plan?

The mention of أَنْعَامِكُمْ (your cattle) specifically teaches that we have responsibilities toward animals. They depend on the same provisions we do, and Allah provides for them through us. This creates an ethic of compassion and stewardship toward all creatures.

Balance in Life

The list of provisions includes both necessities (قَضْبًا - vegetables, نَخْلًا - palm trees) and luxuries (عِنَبًا - grapes), both sustenance (طَعَام) and beauty (فَاكِهَةً - fruits, حَدَائِقَ - gardens). This teaches that a balanced life includes both.

Don’t become so ascetic that you reject Allah’s beautiful provisions. Don’t become so focused on luxuries that you forget basic needs and responsibilities. Appreciate both utility and beauty, both function and form, both necessity and enhancement.

This applies to how we build our lives, our communities, and our relationship with Allah. We need the practical necessities of faith (prayer, fasting, charity) and also the beautifying elements (good character, joy in worship, appreciation of creation). We need the vegetables and the grapes, the olives and the palm trees.

The Ultimate Reality: It All Comes Back to Belief

Despite all the social, economic, and relational complexities of life, the surah ends with the simplest division: الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ (disbelievers who did as they pleased) versus those with bright, joyful faces.

The wisdom is that ultimately, only one thing matters: Did you believe and submit, or did you reject and rebel? Everything else - your wealth, status, intelligence, beauty, lineage - will be irrelevant on Judgment Day. The only question is: Were you among those who recognized truth and lived by it, or were you among those who rejected truth and lived by your own desires?

This should clarify our priorities. We spend so much time and energy on things that won’t matter - pursuing status, accumulating wealth, impressing people, building reputations. The wisdom of these ayahs is to redirect that energy toward what actually matters: building our relationship with Allah, living according to His revelation, and preparing for the inevitable accountability.

The blessed faces are bright because they’re in Allah’s presence - they sought Him in this life, and they found Him in the next. The wretched faces are dark because they’re separated from Allah - they rejected Him in this life, and they’re cut off from Him in the next. This is the ultimate reality that should govern all our choices.
